[[RunningHead]]

Integration: The Old and the New

Progress in Psychoanalysis: Envisioning the Future of the Profession. By Steven D. Axelrod, Ronald C. Naso, and Larry M. Rosenberg. New York: Routledge, 2018, xxii + 305 pp., $140.00 hardcover, $44.95 paperback.

David Cooper

David Cooper

5480 Wisconsin Avenue, #225
Chevy Chase, MD 20815
E-mail: dcooperphd@gmail.com

David Cooper   Training Analyst, Washington Baltimore Psychoanalytic Institute; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences, George Washington University School of Medicine and Health Sciences.

I was talking with a friend recently about a mutual acquaintance, also in the field. “He's progressive but not crazy,” my friend said in sharing my appreciation of our colleague. In his comment, he reflected something I think many of us struggle with: how do we help the field of psychoanalysis to progress (or be progressive) without throwing out valuable pieces of our past and valuable lessons taught by those who came before us? This need for change, to bring our theories and practice into the contemporary scene in a useful way, is something that has engaged my thinking and my teaching for quite a while, so I was pleased to be asked to review Axelrod, Naso, and Rosenberg's Progress in Psychoanalysis: Envisioning the Future of the Profession.

The tension around balancing the old and the new runs throughout this volume. In his call in chapter 1 for a “strong pluralism,” Elliot Jurist takes on the problem that, by retaining too much of the past alongside new ideas, one can, as David Lichtenstein notes, “lapse into an incoherent pluralism” (p. 80). This collection of papers demonstrates that there are many areas in which psychoanalysts are actively engaged in doing things differently, including the critiques of past ways of engaging with contemporary problems. I do think they also miss some signs of progress within psychoanalytic organizations, which I will reference.

That psychoanalysis is a complex, multiply tasked field complicates the marking of progress. Are we talking about theory? Practice? Training? Applications? Relationships with other intellectual and healthcare fields? Its place in contemporary intellectual life? Its willingness to be receptive to differences within the field and among disparate fields?

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editor's Introduction

Mitchell Wilson

Mitchell Wilson

E-mail: mdwmd57@cloud.com

Mitchell Wilson  

For JAPA 67/6 we are privileged to publish Otto Kernberg's “Therapeutic Implications of Transference Structures in Various Personality Pathologies.” This paper, a tour de force of conceptual rigor and stylistic clarity, is in large part a summary statement of Kernberg's multi-decade research program on the nature of personality structure and organization, especially their pathological formations, and the psychotherapeutic treatment implications that follow from that work. The history of psychoanalysis in the United States, as many are well aware, in large measure developed in tandem with academic medicine and psychiatry. In the 1950s through the 1970s, some eminent departments of psychiatry were led by academically minded psychoanalysts, and major research projects of significance flourished in that context. The Menninger Psychotherapy Research Project (Wallerstein 1986), to which, early in his career, Kernberg was a major contributor, is perhaps the best known example of empirical work combining a psychoanalytic orientation and painstaking methodology in an institutional setting. While the professional landscape has changed rather dramatically over the ensuing decades—signaled by the advent of DSM-III (see Wilson 1993)—and professional divisions between psychoanalysts and academic psychiatry have widened appreciably, Kernberg has continued his pursuit of a detailed examination of personality pathologies and their treatment from a psychoanalytic point of view. His influence has been vast, and certainly not only on the various research groups that continue to explore the details of personality disorders. His creatively bringing together American ego psychology and British object relations traditions has had a lasting impact on the psychoanalytic education of candidates and the training of psychiatric residents.

While it is impossible to fully capture the breadth and significance of Otto Kernberg's contributions to mental health research and treatment, we hope that in this issue of JAPA the reader will get more than a sense of what he has meant to the field.

[[RunningHead]]

From the Editor

Mitchell Wilson

Mitchell Wilson

Mitchell Wilson  

It is an honor to have been chosen to be the new Editor of JAPA and to help shepherd the journal into the next decade. The knowledge base of a profession is its heart and soul—it gives all of us reasons for doing what we do, whether it be scholarly work or clinical practice, or, often enough, both. Our knowledge base is an open, living, and evolving entity, a fertile source of dialogue and debate. JAPA has been a key player in psychoanalytic knowledge production since 1953, first functioning as the “scientific” arm of the American Psychoanalytic Association and gradually evolving—through years of inspired editorial leadership by John Frosch, Harold Blum, Ted Shapiro, Arnie Richards, Steve Levy, and Bonnie Litowitz—into a journal of impressive intellectual and geographic influence.

I am taking over editorial responsibilities from Bonnie Litowitz, who has led JAPA for the past five years with a remarkable blend of intelligence, integrity, and care. She has enlivened the journal with an ethos of intellectual open-mindedness and purposeful engagement with scholars from other disciplines and clinicians who in the past, perhaps, were kept outside the psychoanalytic pale. She has expanded JAPA's reach into Asian countries via translations of key papers, augmented JAPA's podcast offerings, and curated unusual content, such as Julia Kristeva's “Maternal Eroticism” (62/1, 2014)—efforts that, collectively, have given JAPA more theoretical dimensionality and scholarly and clinical impact (such efforts will be continued under my tenure). Bonnie has also used JAPA's relationship with Sage Publishing to contact growing numbers of readers and listeners through digital platforms. Those of us who have worked closely with her have learned a great deal, and I cannot imagine being in my current position without the benefit of her generous and wise mentorship.

In my estimation, we have as a collective become quite good at espousing particular, if not entirely idiosyncratic, points of view; unfortunately, we have also become less able to debate and engage in meaningful conversation with contrary points of view.

Introduction: Roy Schafer's “Problems in Freud's Psychology of Women”

Gerald Fogel

Gerald Fogel

2665 SW Sherwood Drive
Portland, OR 97201-1616
E-mail: geraldfogel@comcast.net

Gerald Fogel  

When Mitchell Wilson asked me to write this introduction, we agreed to keep two goals in mind. First, how might we place Roy Schafer's 1974 paper, “Problems in Freud's Psychology of Women,” in its immediate historical context? What had been going on in the author's work and in analytic theory and practice at the time the paper was written, and how was it received? Second, how might the paper be placed in the entirety of Schafer's work? For more than sixty years he produced creative, elegantly written papers on a multitude of psychoanalytic topics. How did his work evolve and change over those years, and how did the analytic world change during that time?

In a personal memoir, “The Author's Odyssey: You Can Get Here from There,” Schafer (2009) describes the course of his intellectual development over a long and productive life in psychoanalysis. Earlier he had concluded that “the red thread running through all [his] writings is investigating the nature of interpretation;” in retrospect, however, he now had discovered “a second red thread: conflict between a powerful wish to accept, master, and if possible enhance received wisdom and an equally powerful inclination to challenge that heritage and find it wanting” (p. 156).

In “Problems in Freud's Psychology of Women” Schafer is extremely critical of Freud's published views on women, and finds them seriously wanting. Rosemary Balsam (2013) tells us that Schafer's paper had an immediate and profound impact on the analytic world.

Training and Supervising Analyst and founder and former Director, Oregon Psychoanalytic Institute.

Psychoanalysis and the World in Turmoil

Mind, Culture, and Global Unrest: Psychoanalytic Reflections. By Salman Akhtar. New York: Routledge, 2018, xiv + 233 pp., $160.00 hardcover, $37.95 paperback.

Marcia Cavell

Marcia Cavell

11 Mountain Laurel Lane, #109
Rhinebeck, NY 12572
E-mail: mcavell9@gmail.com

Marcia Cavell   Associate Professor of Philosophy Emerita, Purchase College, State University of New York; CORST graduate, San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute.

As psychoanalysts well know, an understanding of culture and the external world, particularly in these troubled times, requires an understanding of the internal world; but equally, an understanding of the internal world requires an understanding of culture and the external world, which psychoanalysis has to some extent neglected. The embeddedness of mind and culture goes both ways. Such is the thesis of Salman Akhtar's timely and highly informative new book. In his own introductory words:

This book emerges from two motives. The first pertains to my wish to say something—both as an ordinary citizen and as a psychoanalyst—about the current global turmoil. The second involves my desire to expand the purview of my discipline, psychoanalysis, by forging links between the internal world it has mapped out so well and the external reality it has only lately come to recognize as important [p. xiii].

The second exploration he calls “anthropological psychoanalysis” as distinct from “psychoanalytic anthropology,” which he maintains is informed too much by Western conceptions of, for example, child-rearing, parent-child relationships, masculinity and femininity, and autonomy. Akhtar credits Freud with the dual insight that culture and repression are outgrowths of each other. (In my words: what is repressed in the superego, for example, are not only aggression and forbidden desires, but also the cultural taboos that are integral to the internalized father.)

The book is dense with political and cultural information, perhaps too much for the average psychoanalytic reader, and I will focus on only five of its seven chapters.

545

Mauro Fornaro   Professor of Dynamic Psychology, Gabriele D'Annunzio Univesity of Chieti-Pescara (Italy).

In Half Prisoner, Half Winged, Riccardo Lombardi observes that in psychoanalysis there is only a “rhapsodic” (i.e., infrequent) interest in the body-mind relationship, and proposes a more insistent focus on it, both in theory and in practice, a focus that aims at the integration of mind and body. This book consists of nine short chapters, interspersed with several clinical examples taken from the author's practice or from that of his supervisees. At the end there is an appendix: “Historical Note on the Body-Mind Relationship in Psychoanalysis.”

Editors' note: The English translation of this book (Lombardi 2017) was reviewed by Janice Lieberman in JAPA 66/3 (2018). We believe Lombardi's book is so complex, its topic so vital for our field, that it deserves both reviews; each is excellent, and each offers a distinct perspective. The present review was translated from the author's Italian by Paolo Migone.

554

[[RunningHead]]

An Empirical Perspective on the Transference in Psychotherapy: Commentary on Kernberg

Kevin Meehan

Kevin Meehan

1 University Plaza
Brooklyn, NY 11201
E-mail: kevin.meehan@liu.edu

Kevin Meehan  

My aim here is to consider Kernberg's “Therapeutic Implications of Transference Structures in Various Personality Pathologies” from the vantage point of psychodynamic psychotherapy research. That Kernberg's work lends itself to evaluation from an empirical perspective speaks to the ways in which he is uniquely situated in the psychoanalytic community.

More than any major psychoanalytic theorist, Kernberg has distinguished himself by creating the circumstances under which his clinical perspective may be subject to empirical evaluation. In collaboration with colleagues, Kernberg has manualized his treatment model (Yeomans, Clarkin, and Kernberg 2015). Unlike psychotherapy manuals that dictate the focus of given sessions, such as those typically used in CBT approaches, Kernberg and colleagues have articulated the broad strokes of the tactics, techniques, and strategies of a transference-focused treatment approach to be applied flexibly according to the nature of the patient's pathology and the dynamics of the patient/therapist dyad. They have subjected this treatment to empirical evaluation; through several randomized clinical trials (RCTs), transference-focused psychotherapy (TFP) has demonstrated its efficacy in the treatment of borderline personality disorder (BPD) (Clarkin et al. 2007; Doering et al. 2010).

In an RCT comparing TFP to treatment by experienced community psychotherapists, TFP demonstrated significantly greater reductions in BPD symptoms and inpatient admissions, and greater gains in patients' level of personality organization and psychosocial functioning (Doering et al. 2010).

Associate Professor, Department of Psychology and Ph.D. Program in Clinical Psychology, Long Island University-Brooklyn; Associate Editor, Psychoanalytic Psychology.

987

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Exploring Kernberg's Model of Personality Functioning as a Moderator of Traits: Focus on DSM-5's Section III Alternative Model of Personality Disorder

James Sexton , Margaret Hilton , Stephanie Benson and Adam Rosen

Adam Rosen

135 Old Main
Washington, DC 20052-0086
E-mail: drsexton@gmail.com

Adam Rosen  

personality, traits, functioning, AMPD, five factor

A current trend emphasizes simplified models of traits and personality functioning that would reduce individuals to as few as five potential traits and one dimension of personality functioning. However, the evidence behind those models is based on linear methods of analysis that authors from Cattell to Kernberg (2016) believe do not capture the potential moderating interaction effects of personality. Using models of traits and functioning from the DSM-5, this study demonstrates not only that traits and functioning interact, but that they do so in a rich and meaningful way that points up the dangers of collapsing these concepts. Without taking the interaction into account, satisfaction in romantic or work relationships often could not be predicted or understood. If we cannot predict satisfaction in these relationships with precision, honest confusion arises over how to guide others toward these goals. Low Detachment depends on high levels of Self-Definition, just as low Antagonism relies on high levels of Empathy to be successful. Most interestingly, negative traits and low functioning can actually lead to individual satisfaction, suggesting not only that interaction is key but that nonlinear effects may also be present.

Saving Talk Therapy: How Health Insurers, Big Pharma, and Slanted Science Are Ruining Good Mental Health Care. By Enrico Gnaulati. Boston: Beacon Press, 2018, xii + 255 pp., $27.95 hardcover, $18.00 paperback.

Elliot Jurist

Elliot Jurist

96 Fifth Avenue, Suite 1C
New York, NY 10011
E-mail: ejurist5@gmail.com

Elliot Jurist   Professor of Psychology and Philosophy, The City College of New York and The Graduate Center, The City University of New York; Former Editor, Psychoanalytic Psychology.

Have you ever had a well-educated patient who, it turns out, is clueless about what distinguishes your training as a psychologist or psychiatrist? Have you ever had a patient request that you use cognitive-behavioral techniques because he or she has read that they provide the most effective psychotherapy? Have you ever been told that psychodynamic psychotherapy is old-school, and that no evidence exists to support its efficacy? Has a patient ever expressed the wish that you try to conduct yourself like a life coach? The public is often unaware and even ignorant about the mental health profession, and we ought to recognize that psychoanalysts are especially imperiled by the mounting confusion about treatment options.

Enrico Gnaulati's Saving Talk Therapy: How Health Insurers, Big Pharma, and Slanted Science Are Ruining Good Mental Health Care is a courageous attempt to take stock and to reflect on the current state of mental health treatment. Gnaulati recalls the popular writings of psychoanalysts from an earlier era who defended the value of psychotherapy without jargon or technical language, and hopes to follow in their footsteps in claiming that talk therapy remains a valid way to enhance our sense of agency and to live more authentically and lovingly. Bucking major trends in the world of mental health, Gnaulati takes a stand against how “the entire enterprise of seeing a shrink has been shrunk” (p. 4).

There are three prongs to Gnaulati's critique, following the subtitle of the book: insurance companies, pharmaceutical companies, and bad science. The case against insurance companies focuses on “managed care,” an allegedly cost-conscious strategy that in reality has functioned to limit reimbursement. Underfunding of psychotherapy has made the relation between providers and insurers adversarial. Insurance companies require diagnoses for reimbursement and reflexively try to limit or deny treatment. What they never seem motivated to do, as Gnaulati notes, is to engage in a genuine, clinically rich dialogue about the best interests of the patient (p. 92).

JAPA Prize Announcements

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association

JAPA Prize

The $1000 JAPA Prize is awarded to the author of the best paper published in the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association during the previous year. All publications, including review articles, research articles, essays and book essays, may be considered for the award. Candidates for the award are nominated by the members of the JAPA Editorial Board, with the final decision being made by the Editor-in-Chief.

JAPA New Author Prize

JAPA's $500 New Author Prize recognizes significant contributions to the journal by new authors. It is awarded for the best published manuscript by a new author, and is presented at the Winter Meetings of the American Psychoanalytic Association.

A new author is considered to be someone who has not previously published in JAPA or any other major psychoanalytic journal and whose psychoanalytic career is in its early stages. A mentoring/editorial group has been established to review submissions by new authors and assist in developing manuscripts for publication in JAPA.

Manuscripts must be submitted via the JAPA submission process, using the online system at http://mc.manuscriptcentral.com/japa. At the time of submission, authors should indicate in the cover letter that the paper is to be considered for the New Author Prize, and may ask that it be directed to the mentoring/editorial group for assistance in manuscript development. Final decision about publication remains with the Editorial Board and the Editor-in-Chief, as does selection of the annual prize-winning paper.

Mourning Freud. By Madelon Sprengnether. New York: Bloomsbury Academic, 2018, x + 269 pp., $29.95 paperback.

Jeffrey Berman

Jeffrey Berman

Albany, NY 12222

Jeffrey Berman   Distinguished Teaching Professor of English, University at Albany, State University of New York.

The deliberate ambiguity in the title of Madelon Sprengnether's new book, Mourning Freud, alerts us to her double focus. Mourning Freud explores Freud's experiences and theories of mourning along with the ways in which the post-Freudian age has mourned one of the giants in the history of ideas. Neither a Freud idealizer nor a Freud basher, Sprengnether offers an intriguing discussion of one of the major areas of psychoanalytic theory—and a problem in life we will all encounter.

Sprengnether is well qualified to undertake this ambitious project. A poet, memoirist, and literary critic, she is Regents Professor Emerita of English at the University of Minnesota. Sprengnether is a graduate of the New Directions Program in Psychoanalytic Thinking in Washington, DC, and a member of many regional and national psychoanalytic organizations. She also blogs for Psychology Today. Sprengnether's influential 1990 book, The Spectral Mother: Freud, Feminism, and Psychoanalysis, began her investigations of how and why Freud's theorizing of the oedipus complex obscured a deeper drama that he failed to consider: the preoedipal stage of human development. Freud's inability to theorize early development betrays his fear, in Sprengnether's view, of a spectral, haunting, and often threatening mother.

Sprengnether begins her introduction, “Insight and Blindness,” with an apt quote by the literary theorist Terry Eagleton: a “work's insights, as with all writing, are deeply related to its blindnesses: what it does not say, and how it does not say it, may be as important as what it articulates” (p. 1). The introduction's title may also be an allusion to Paul de Man's Blindness and Insight (1971), a classic of deconstruction. Sprengnether, well versed in psychoanalysis, deconstructionism, and feminism, brings to Freudian studies an uncommon knowledge of “high theory” and a notable capacity for close reading.

Sidney Phillips   Associate Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Yale School of Medicine; Training and Supervising Analyst, Western New England Institute for Psychoanalysis.

This intriguing and unusual book is the product of a major contributor to the psychoanalytic field. Donald Moss has written over sixty published articles, and this is his fourth book, if we don't count a collection of short stories that came out early in his career. This book doesn't read like a short story or an essay or really even a poem. Maybe it's more like a series of Zen koans, statements used to test the progress in Zen practice. The koan is meant to stir greater and greater degrees of uncertainty and self-doubt, even to the point of desperation, such that the student eventually realizes there is no meaning to the koan except what the student attributes to it. The student may come to realize that the desperation and self-doubt too are constructed in the mind to protect against being alive, awake, and emotionally and spiritually present in the moment.1

For one year, Moss wrote down one sentence spoken by his patients, one from each session. Nothing more. No context. No reply. No commentary. Here he presents the selected sentences day by day. When I heard Moss present this work at a scientific meeting at my local analytic society,2 he began with an illuminating introduction. I regret he has not included it in the book. It offered an essential foundation for understanding the selected sentences that follow. Without it, the reader is left to wonder what this book is all about. I speculate later in this review about its omission. I cannot undo having heard it and thought about it, so I include a description and commentary on his oral introduction.

1This is a lay interpretation of Zen practice. I claim no firsthand experience of it. I use the koan as a metaphor for inducing a deeper, skeptical search within.2He presented a shortened version of this work at a scientific meeting of the Western New England Psychoanalytic Society in New Haven on December 17, 2016. Alfred Margulies and I were discussants.563

Stephanie Brody approaches her extended meditation on loss, mortality, and psychoanalysis through the lens of storytelling. The genesis of the book emerged from an unlikely place. Brody enrolled in a community education class on the Odyssey with Dr. Michael Fiveash, a recently retired teacher at the public high school in Lexington, Massachusetts.

She found him an inspired and inspiring teacher, “simply the best teacher I had ever encountered” (p. 1). Brody took four classes with Fiveash, in which she found a resonance between stories in the tradition of the classics and psychoanalysis:

The concepts of storytelling, the oral tradition, and the development of character, the effort to achieve a life of kelos—the Greek word for a noble reputation—a kind of immortality, felt familiar. That much of life was decided in the betwixt and between of liminality, that we heroically seek change despite impossible obstacles and limits, that we are often alone, but seek intimate companionship, seemed to reflect the psychoanalytic world that was my daily habitat” [p. 1].

Brody had previously published two of the chapters included in this volume, and as she began to construct a framework for the book, she imagined that Dr. Fiveash would serve as her consultant and guide. Two weeks after she signed the book contract, however, he became very ill and died. “That I decided to go forward anyway could only be regarded as an act of personal necessity: I wrote in a fever of mourning” (p. 2). An elegiac tone pervades the book. Let me say here that I believe Brody is making an assumption, rather than a strong case, regarding the resonance between storytelling in the classic vein and psychoanalysis. I feel that Brody has written two books, which run in parallel. One is about the effect on her, and on her psychoanalytic thinking, of her immersion in these stories. The other is a more traditional book about loss and resilience in psychoanalysis, as her subtitle suggests. These two projects sit somewhat uneasily side by side.

Educators without formal training at an analytic institute may find it challenging to introduce and integrate psychoanalytic ideas and techniques into mental health curricula. A number of factors contribute to this difficulty, including students' need for a semantic foundation allowing their comprehension of psychoanalytic jargon; the many distinctive theoretical approaches that historically have been shrouded in conflict; a paucity of evidence-based studies demonstrating efficacy; and the lack of a model of therapeutic action that is sufficiently user-friendly for the larger mental health community (Yalof 2015). In the short volume Freedom to Choose, Jack and Kerry Kelly Novick begin to address many of these issues as they present their novel approach to introducing psychoanalytic theory and technique, drawing on their experience as researchers, theoreticians, clinicians, teachers, and supervisors. Published in 2016, Freedom to Choose introduces students interested in clinical psychoanalysis to the Novicks' synthesis of their evolving ideas over five decades into a systematized approach to contemporary psychoanalytic theory and practice.

Influenced by the field of developmental psychopathology, which studies normal and abnormal development, risk and resilience factors, and the concept of adaptive and maladaptive outcomes, the authors describe two general pathways of self-regulation by which individuals can fulfill the basic “need for homeostasis and mastery, which in turn underlie a sense of self and self-esteem” (p. 3). Their use of the term “self-regulation,” rather than more psychoanalytic phrases such as “affective regulation,” facilitates the integration of psychoanalytic ideas into the wider universe of mental health (Kopp 1982; Cicchetti, Ackerman, and Izard 1995). Self-regulation has become a particular focus in the field of developmental psychology over the last twenty-five years and is now considered an early childhood marker for later successes (Montroy et al. 2016). Even more important, it is commonly used in every mental health discipline as a descriptive term and is frequently listed as a goal of clinical treatment.

[[RunningHead]]

Writing Psychoanalysis

Warren Poland

Warren Poland

5225 Connecticut Avenue
Washington, DC 20015
E-mail: warrenpoland@gmail.com

Warren Poland  

Literature, Flaubert said, is a perpetual orgy. For many that may not be so, yet for some it is ever true. How has it been that so vital a part of life is slighted by psychoanalytic attention? Such little recognition of the place of passion for language in shaping a person's mind is particularly ironic in an endeavor actualized through talk, a field brought to life by a great and gifted writer.

Psychoanalysts know well the centrality of the body in a mind's coming into being, the ego “first and foremost, a bodily ego,” as Freud declared (1923, p. 26). We recognize the significance when we see a contented baby at its mother's breast look up to its mother's eyes. We understand when we watch an infant's fascination with the hand at the end of its outstretched arm, discovering its power to move that hand. In our study of the place of the body in a child's coming into being, however, too much do we leave language acquisition as if lost on the side.

For some, learning words, learning to use and play with words, is as exciting and significant as any other growing power. The several stages of bodily investment we group under the rubric of sexuality and deem uniquely central in a child's self-definition may be too narrow a view.

Sensuality might be a more fitting word, one that includes sexuality but leaves room open for the love, indeed the passion, for language that marks the character of many who become ardent as both readers and writers. The import of body and that of language overlap, with even the ideas in words early involving physical pleasure. In an unpublished talk, the poet Robert Pinsky offered an illustration. He reminisced about his delight in the sensation of words in his mouth while a child, such as his experience of the crackling texture of words like “knuckle.” For some children, a love affair with words has full force as part of their growing love affair with the world. I know firsthand of what Pinsky spoke.

Former Editor, JAPA Review of Books.

Adam Natoli  

DSM-5, personality, personality disorders, Alternative Model for Personality Disorders

Often believed to have Kraepelinian origins, the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders—5th Edition (DSM-5) defines personality disorders using a categorical, hierarchical taxonomic system. This system possesses many long-standing problems for clinical practice, including a large assortment of symptom combinations that contribute to problematic heterogeneity and likely impair diagnostic validity. The DSM diagnostic system was at one time heavily influenced by psychoanalytic theory (Shorter 2005). A desire for greater theoretical neutrality then encouraged a shift away from psychoanalytic theory, resulting in the problematic atheoretical model of personality pathology introduced in DSM-III (1980) and still used today. The Alternative Model for Personality Disorders (AMPD), introduced in DSM-5 (2013), is an attempt to reconcile many of the categorical model's issues and directly parallels primary themes that characterize psychoanalytic models of personality. After a review of the historical development of DSM, three current systems for diagnosing personality pathology—the DSM-5's categorical model (2013), its AMPD (2013), and the Psychodynamic Diagnostic Manual (2nd ed.; Alliance of Psychoanalytic Organizations 2017) are compared. The comparison illustrates how the AMPD brings psychoanalytic theory back into the DSM system and acknowledges the implications of a more psychoanalytic DSM.

Commentary on Abbasi

Adrienne Harris

Adrienne Harris

80 University Place, Fifth Floor
New York, NY 10003-4564
E-mail: adrienneharris@gmail.com

Adrienne Harris  

Aisha Abbasi has offered us a moving and challenging case report. I see that I mean moving in various senses of the word: the treatment migrates, and the emotions flowing within the treatment are extremely poignant. The treatment is long and winding. There is migration, return to home, a spiral process between India and America as the treatment deepens and takes its astonishing turns (as treatments that will work surely must).

I start at the beginning with the earthquake and its moments of confusion and recognition. This is a point of contact that reverberates. The symbol of the earthquake becomes increasingly saturated. Beginning in a fact of weather and fearful upheaval, we come to understand the earthquakes in the wars of India and Pakistan, in the trauma to the analyst's father and to the analysand. These traumas, though different, overlap: an earthquake in 1947 with Partition (between India and Pakistan), the 1971 Indo-Pakistani war in which the analyst's father is taken prisoner, and the earthquake of the patient's sexuality, the eruption of desire that shakes his world, requiring flight, return, more flight, and then some settling.

The challenge in Abbasi's paper is twofold. We are asked to consider carefully the analytic implications of new technologies—our work with devices such as phones and Skype. These experiences all require that we confront our often unexamined definitions and constructions of the analytic frame. This analytic couple made complex and multiple uses of the setting for their work, which I understand using Bleger's enlarged definition of the frame (1967). Throughout the work, the analyst attended to the stability of the setting as the ground on which analytic insights into the work would emerge. She was actively engaged in understanding both the transference and the countertransference as these processes were informed by the various settings and their meanings.

What I would say is unique in this report is the gradual and inexorable process in which the setting becomes part of it, as the unique use of devices (phones, Skype) enters the transference and countertransference.

Commentary on Abbasi

Dominique Scarfone

Dominique Scarfone

1430 Redpath Crescent
Montreal, QC H3G 1A2
CANADA
E-mail: dscarfone@gmail.com

Dominique Scarfone  

The ways in which one can comment on a wonderful piece of clinical work such as this are many, and since Aisha Abbasi's presentation makes her patient M. come so much alive, it is tempting to piggyback on her insightful understanding of the case and say a few things I hope will add to our understanding. Moreover, there are elements in the paper that are especially appealing to me, notably the mother's seductive stance, the idea of contamination, and the reference to earthquakes and terrorist attacks. These are but a few of the many fascinating keys in which to read this case material from one's preferred perspective, but tempting as this may be, I will leave this aspect for later. What I wish to highlight first is what this paper has taught me and what change it has brought in me, as every good paper should.

A first important element, not entirely new to me, has now entered my set of beliefs thanks to Abbasi's convincing example. It is the belief that the patients we take into analysis, at least those we end up talking or writing about, frequently have something significant in common with our personal story. It seems that it is rarely by chance that we present one case rather than another, except of course when confidentiality issues are at stake.

The second thing I learned from this case narrative is that until now I had a rather simplistic approach to the question of long-distance analysis by phone or Skype. In view of what goes missing in sessions thus conducted, I doubted they could still be called analysis. I accord such great importance to the idea of embodiment, to the nonverbal experience, to the minute vibrations in the communication between patient and analyst, to the way in which body posture affects the analytic exchange, and things of the sort that I felt, until now, that long-distance analysis posed a real challenge, to say the least.

Honorary Professor, Université de Montréal; Training and Supervising Analyst, Canadian Psychoanalytic Institute, Montreal French branch.

Stanley Possick   Faculty, Western New England Institute for Psychoanalysis; Associate Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Yale School of Medicine.

The roles of psychoanalytic, psychodynamic, and other psychotherapeutic approaches to the treatment of schizophrenia have evolved in a number of ways since Emil Kraepelin's 1890s description of “Dementia Praecox as a brain process of unknown causation, leading to demented end states” (Alanen, chapter 1 in Alanen et al. 2009, p. 4). Eugen Bleuler (1911), who coined the term schizophrenia for dementia praecox, disagreed with Kraepelin about the eventual outcome of schizophrenic disorders. He believed that not all schizophrenic patients progress to dementia, and argued that the disorder is not due entirely to organic causes. Bleuler described the “primary” symptoms of schizophrenia—“autism and disintegration of cognitive and affective functions—as being most probably due to organic causes, but attributed the content of the ‘secondary’ symptoms—including delusions and hallucinations—to the patient's individual complexes” (p. 4). Bleuler believed that secondary symptoms such as delusions and hallucinations could be treated with psychoanalysis.

Theories of Development

The Analyst's Experience of the Depressive Position: The Melancholic Errand of Psychoanalysis. By Steven H. Cooper. New York: Routledge, 2016, viii + 175 pp., $190.00 hardcover, $47.95 paperback.

Jane Kite

Jane Kite

209 Pearl Street
Cambridge, MA 02139-4015
E-mail: jvkite@gmail.com

Jane Kite   Training and Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute.

A story's meaning is definable only after scrutinizing the particular manner in which it has been told.

—Roy Schafer (1958, p. 181)

Put another way, psychoanalyses are conducted by human beings.

—Steven H. Cooper (2016, p. 1)

It has taken me an unconscionably long time to get started on this review of a book I have found mostly brilliant and always helpful. Why? I think it has something to do with how the story is told. On first reading, the book appears to be an intricate and often recursive mix of theory and personal and professional development, with special attention paid to repetition, disappointment, and “the inevitability of not working through some conflicts” (p. 47). On returning to the book, however, the reader notices that this isn't simply a narrative of analytic development with an emphasis on a kind of necessary disillusionment. Embedded in Steven Cooper's argument is an erudite manifesto calling for the interrogation of any and all psychoanalytic theories for their specific clinical utility, and, less obviously, the way in which they speak to the developing analyst personally. The analyst himself is the hidden figure in this book, endlessly inquisitive and endlessly (and reassuringly) uncertain.

Noting in his introduction that he has never been very fond of the term “depressive position” and adopting Winnicott's faute de mieux characterization of the term as “a bad name for a normal process” (p. 3), Cooper orients us at once to the importance of multiple theoretical models converging on his own “normal process” development as an analyst.

Aisha Abbasi   Training and Supervising Analyst, Michigan Psychoanalytic Institute.

It is rare for a book review to start with the sentence “I loved this book.” I am, however, going to take the liberty of starting this review with exactly that sentence. I loved this book. What a useful and lovely idea Robert Winer and Kerry Malawista had when they envisioned this project! They asked twenty-five analysts to answer a set of thirty-two questions they had put together for an interview with each analyst. Only twenty-one agreed to take part. After the interviews were transcribed, two subjects felt that too much was exposed in their interview and asked asked that it not be printed. Two others were not included for reasons not shared with us. Thus, the book contains seventeen interviews, with eleven male analysts and six female analysts, from the U.S. and several other nations.

Why did Winer and Malawista take on this project? They wanted, it seems, to explore various questions posed in their introduction: “What is it like to be a working psychoanalyst? … What do analysts experience in the course of meeting with their patients? How do they think about what they are doing? What is it like for them to take account of themselves? … To feel appreciated and then to feel devalued? To feel betrayed? To try to find ways to do what's possible? To feel responsibility for someone's life while working to maintain your own balance?” (p. xv).

Another reason is their realization that often in psychoanalysis case presentations are “so impersonal”: “neither the patient nor the analyst comes to life… .

Couples on the Couch: Psychoanalytic Couple Therapy and the Tavistock Model. Edited by Shelley Nathans and Milton Schaefer. New York: Routledge, 2017, xxvi + 261, $195.00 hardcover, $50.95 paperback.

Richard Zeitner

Richard Zeitner

1007 SW Walnut Street
Blue Springs, MO 64015
E-mail: richzeitner@gmail.com

Richard Zeitner   Training and Supervising Analyst and faculty, Greater Kansas City Psychoanalytic Institute; Clinical Assistant Professor of Psychiatry and Psychology, University of Missouri-Kansas City School of Medicine; adjunct faculty, International Psychotherapy Institute; North American Co-chair, Subcommittee on Family and Couple Psychoanalysis (COFAP), International Psychoanalytical Association.

Couples on the Couch: Psychoanalytic Couple Therapy and the Tavistock Model, edited by Shelley Nathans and Milton Schaefer, is a creatively edited compilation of papers undertaken by two research and teaching groups—the Tavistock Centre for Couple Relationships (TCCR, now called Tavistock Relationships, or TR), located in London, and the Psychoanalytic Couple Psychotherapy Group (PCPG), located in Berkeley, California.

To provide some history, approximately two decades ago a group of dedicated psychoanalytically informed clinicians in the Bay Area made contact with TR—an institute that had pioneered in developing theories and methods for the treatment of disturbed couple and group relationships, rooted in psychoanalytic thinking, especially the object relations tradition. As I understand the origins of this partnership, these California clinicians, all seasoned in the psychoanalytic tradition and wanting to learn more about the Tavistock application to couples, traveled to London to study the developments occurring there. Later the TR clinicians were invited to Berkeley to teach and supervise the Bay Area clinicians. Emerging from these repeated sojourns was a relationship between the two groups, eventuating in the founding of PCPG. Since then PCPG has continued to expand upon the Tavistock tradition, while continuing its collaboration with TR.

Here I will say something about the Tavistock model and its history, especially for those less familiar with its approach in the treatment of couples. Beginning in the 1940s, Enid Balint, a London psychoanalyst, was instrumental in developing the Family Discussion Bureau, a precursor of TR.

705

[[RunningHead]]

Exorcising the Demons

Glen Gabbard

Glen Gabbard

4306 Yoakum Boulevard, Suite 535
Houston, TX 77006
E-mail: glen.gabbard@gmail.com

Glen Gabbard  

About a decade ago, the late Ethel Person and I sat down for a cup of coffee and discussed why we wrote. Ethel noted that she wrote to figure out what she thought. To me, this perspective rang true, but incomplete. I think we also write to find out what others think. Writing a publishable psychoanalytic paper requires one to read and study before writing. After perusing the literature, one hopes to arrive at some form of mastery over a particular aspect of human experience. While this approach certainly makes sense, I have come to the disappointing conclusion that the mastery one seeks is partial at best. Much of analytic data arrives in fragments from a session here and a session there. Writing brings some degree of coherence to the fragments. However, some of the most penetrating insights lead to further uncertainty. Hence the quest for mastery may be one reason I write, but it is an aspiration for mastery that often lies just beyond my reach.

Another motivation for my long interest in writing is related to the wish to be known and heard. Analysts spend much of their day in a state of reserve, biting their tongues, eschewing chattiness, and avoiding premature conclusions. Such restraint is mitigated to some extent through the act of writing, where one can elaborate on the complexities of psychoanalytic thinking and can express one's views more fully. Like many of my colleagues, I have a need to draw conclusions about human experience, and to share them with others in an effort to understand the staggering complexity of unconscious motivation and the painful truths connected to the self-defeating trends within all of us.

Warren Poland (one of my favorite psychoanalytic writers) launched this new feature on the motivations for writing—Why I Write—in the last issue of JAPA. In his wonderful contribution (Poland 2019), he emphasized the pleasure one derives from writing. I can relate to this pleasure of creation, but my emphasis here is on the darker forces at work when I write.

[[RunningHead]]

A Magic World

Donnei Stern

Donnei Stern

24 East 82nd Street
New York, NY 10028
E-mail: donnelstern@gmail.com

Donnei Stern  

I love psychoanalysis. But my devotion to our literature and my desire to contribute to it, as much as they shape me, are not, at bottom, why I write. But don't misunderstand: without the love of a subject matter, neither I nor anyone else would have any reason to write. To write, you must love to think about something. But I think I would write even if it weren't psychoanalysis I loved.

Many years ago, in the New York Times Book Review, the novelist Joyce Carol Oates (1982) wrote, “If I say that I write with the enormous hope of altering the world—and why write without that hope?—I should first say that I write to discover what it is I will have written” (p. 1). Writing is just this kind of emergent process for me. I can't make it happen, and I have the very clear sense that it would be a shame if I could. I have no choice but simply to observe the unfolding of my own thoughts and feelings on the page. I am an onlooker at processes that, paradoxically, could not be more my own.

When I write, I hope to get out of the way and let something grow and change and come into focus, only to verge back into the unformulated and then begin to emerge again. I want to go through this cycle of experience, which never grows old, again and again. It gives me a feeling like no other, a feeling of becoming, of participating in the life of something bigger than myself, of contact with mystery.

As writers, we hope to bring ourselves so thoroughly to bear on the problem at hand, with so little reservation, holding back so little, that something comes into being at least a little bit differently than it has ever done before. We aim to access something we have always lived with but sensed only hazily, or never perceived at all. Writers, when successful, are midwives of being. We go deep inside ourselves and find … the world!

This is what I know about why I write. Or rather, it's why I couldn't not write. Life is an ongoing project of reinvention: the slippage of signifiers lies at the heart of creation.

What Can I Say? Contested Words and Contested Thoughts in the Contemporary Moment

Michael Garfinkle , Jill Gentile , Bonnie Litowitz , Ann Pellegrini , Lara Sheehi , Mitchell Wilson and Lynne Zeavin

Lynne Zeavin

Lynne Zeavin  

Mitchell Wilson: Introduction

The unusual set of writings you are about to read comes from a panel that Lynne Zeavin and I put together for the APsaA meeting in February 2019 titled “What Can I Say? Contested Words and Contested Thoughts in the Contemporary Moment.” Our idea for the panel arose in response to a moment, some years back, that was both quintessentially psychoanalytic and extremely painful. It was at the APsaA spring meeting in Austin in 2016, during the clinical plenary. In the comment period—after the plenary presentation and the two discussions that followed (I was one of the discussants)—an audience member said forthrightly: “I am greatly distressed. I think the plenary speaker and discussants should be made aware of the damage they have caused by their use of the term homosexual. The history of this word, and the oppressive uses to which it has been put, both inside and outside of psychoanalysis, make it unusable, except as an instrument to do further harm.

[[RunningHead]]

Problems in Freud's Psychology of Women

Roy Schafer

Roy Schafer

Roy Schafer  

Freud's ideas on the development and psychological characteristics of girls and women, though laden with rich clinical and theoretical discoveries and achievements, appear to have been significantly flawed by the influence of traditional patriarchal and evolutionary values. This influence is evident in certain questionable presuppositions, logical errors and inconsistencies, suspensions of intensive inquiry, underemphasis on certain developmental variables, and confusions between observations, definitions, and value preferences. Under three headings—The Problem of Women's Morality and Objectivity, The Problem of Neglected Prephallic Development, and The Problem of Naming—I discuss Freud's generalizations concerning ego and superego development in boys and girls, penis envy, biologically predestined procreativity, the role of the mother, the fateful linkages male-masculine-active-aggressive-dominant and female-feminine-passive-masochistic-submissive, and other topics as well. In general, it is argued that Freud's generalizations concerning girls and women do injustice to both his psychoanalytic method and his clinical findings.

Clinical Reasoning, Grünbaum, and Freud's “Cleverest Dreamer” Dream Interpretation

Michael Michael

Michael Michael

50 Yonsei-ro, Seodaemun-gu
Seoul 03722
SOUTH KOREA
E-mail: mmichael.esq@gmail.com

Michael Michael  

Adolf Grünbaum, Sigmund Freud, counterwish dreams, inference to the best explanation, commonsense psychology

The philosopher Adolf Grünbaum has repeatedly criticized Freud's reasoning, claiming that much of it is overtly fallacious. One such criticism that has gone without reply concerns Freud's controversial response to the counterwish objection to his theory of dreams, that the reason some dreams appear to represent the frustration of a wish rather than the fulfillment of one is that they actually represent the dreamer's wish to prove Freud wrong. Grünbaum contends that in giving such a response Freud commits several glaring fallacies. But Grünbaum's analysis is mistaken and misrepresents Freud's thought. Contrary to Grünbaum's interpretation of Freud's reasoning as deductive, this reasoning is best construed as a form of “inference to the best explanation.”

Relationships in Development: Infancy, Intersubjectivity, and Attachment. By Stephen Seligman. New York: Routledge, 2018, xxx + 339 pp., $145.00 hardcover, $46.95 paperback.

Stephen Atkins

Stephen Atkins

251 Edwards Street
New Haven, CT 06511
E-mail: sratkins@optonline.net

Stephen Atkins   Assistant Clinical Professor, Department of Psychiatry, Yale School of Medicine.

In Relationships in Development Stephen Seligman provides an excellent statement of what he terms “relational developmental psychoanalysis,” which he frequently contrasts with a classical view. For him “a relationship-oriented ‘two-person’ approach and clinical orientations about the analyst's oracular, abstinent position that followed from them: relationships, rather than drives, are the organizers and motivations of psychic life, and the individual and her environments—especially other people—are seen as inextricably intertwined” (p. 4). Moreover, the emphasis of classical theory on the intrapsychic “unduly turns attention from compelling social realities, just as it devalues real trauma” (p. 36).

For Seligman the relationship is primary, and he explores the implications this has for psychoanalytic theory. Seligman sees relationships in the context of development. Basing himself on direct observational research into infant-parent interaction and early infant-caregiver relationships, he states that “infants are born prepared to respond to and evoke care from parents and others; that the creation and maintenance of ties to other people are primary and central motivations for infants … and humans in general; and that the relationship between infants and caregivers is the fundamental unit within which early development occurs” (pp. 4-5).

Seligman challenges what he understands as the classical psychoanalytic view of the baby as “solipsistic and chaotic,” struggling with “primitive, endogenous libidinal and aggressive instincts” (p. 6). Citing Winnicott's notion that “there is no infant without the mother,” he insists the baby be understood as “inextricably engaged with its caregiving surround” (p. 54). Seligman's emphasis is on a natural adaptive dynamic in the meeting of children's physical and psychological growth within a facilitating environment. He finds Freud too focused on individual psychic development without affirming its necessary connection to the mother and the matrix in which development occurs.

From the Editors

Anne Adelman and Jennifer Stuart

Jennifer Stuart

E-mail: anne.adelmanphd@gmail.com

jstuartphd@gmail.com

Jennifer Stuart  

We are pleased to present our first compilation of reviews as the new editors of JAPA Review of Books. It is our privilege to follow Rosemary Balsam and Paul Schwaber, who have led the book review section for fifteen years with thoughtfulness and wisdom, bringing new and important psychoanalytic works to these pages. As editors we hope to continue in their excellent tradition, presenting book reviews and essays that make new ideas accessible and offer fresh perspective on old ideas.

With our inaugural issue, we are delighted to bring something new to these pages. In a column we are calling “Why I Write,” we hope to foster a discussion about the experience of writing as an author for whom psychoanalysis is centrally important. Whether we write about our clinical and theoretical work, personal experience, literature, arts, or politics; whether we produce scholarly works, memoir, or fiction—why do we do it? What drives us to put our thoughts to paper, to engage our minds in the task of writing? What motivates us to write about our work, or about other matters important to us? “Why I Write” will feature authors' personal reflections about the experience of writing, exploring such topics as the process of writing while seeing patients; the mutual influences of writing, treatment (as patient, therapist, or both), and teaching; the complexities of sharing our clinical work on the written page, where both our colleagues and our patients may read about it; and the experience of having our manuscripts in print. We welcome contributions from psychoanalysts and psychoanalytic therapists, and also from academics, writers, and artists for whom psychoanalytic theory—or a personal experience of psychoanalytic treatment—has shaped the work of writing. Please watch for “Why I Write” at the end of each JAPA Review of Books.

From the Editors

Anne Adelman and Jennifer Stuart

Jennifer Stuart

E-mail: anne.adelmanmd@gmail.com

Jennifer Stuart  

In this issue of JAPA Review of Books we build on two themes explored in the original papers by Arthur Nielsen on projective identification and couple therapy, and by Karen Gilmore on development and emerging adulthood.

For those of you who treat couples in analytic therapy—and for those of you who don't, but would welcome a window into this facet of analytic treatment—we are pleased to spotlight two important new contributions to the literature on psychoanalytic couple therapy. Richard Zeitner's review of Shelley Nathans and Milton Schaefer's edited volume, Couples on the Couch, begins with a helpful history of the Psychoanalytic Couple Psychotherapy Group (PCPG, based in Berkeley, California), and of its mooring in the Tavistock tradition of treatment for couples and groups—which endures today at Tavistock Relationships (TR) in London. Both Nathans and Schaefer are on the faculty at PCPG, and as Zeitner notes, most contributors to this volume are affiliated with either PCPG or TR. Collectively, their contributions capture much of current thinking about psychoanalytic work with couples, especially in the TR tradition. Mary Morgan's single-authored A Couple State of Mind also elaborates the TR approach to psychoanalytic couple therapy. In her review of Morgan's book, Caroline Sehon appreciates the author's use of clinical vignettes “artfully selected to illustrate complex dynamics and techniques.” Sehon highlights Morgan's focus on the value of “observing the couple relationship from a ‘third position’;” her use of developmental theory to understand how members of a couple may grow, or fail to grow, together; and her attention to uses of transference and countertransference in the psychoanalytic treatment of couples.

Developmental Theory and Play Therapy

Play Therapy: A Psychodynamic Primer for the Treatment of Young Children. By Pamela Meersand and Karen J. Gilmore. Arlington, VA: American Psychiatric Publishing, 2018, xvi + 468 pp., $78.00 paperback.

Sandra Landen

Sandra Landen

11500 Olympic Boulevard, Suite 625
Los Angeles, CA 90064
E-mail: drsandylanden@gmail.com

Sandra Landen   Faculty, Child and Adolescent Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy Program, New Center for Psychoanalysis.

Psychotherapy takes place in the overlap of two areas of playing, that of the patient and that of the therapist,

—D. W. Winnicott (1971, p. 38)

In Play Therapy: A Psychodynamic Primer for the Treatment of Young Children, Pamela Meersand and Karen Gilmore draw on their decades of clinical practice, supervising, teaching, and writing to provide a twenty-first-century volume that examines the current foundational psychoanalytic principles of play. They consider the developmental arc of play, the central role of play as the primary medium and language of childhood and in treatment, the mutative and growth-promoting aspects of play therapy, and common challenges found in treating children, and finally they offer practical guidance. This primer is an essential read for both child psychotherapists and psychoanalysts beginning practice with young children, as it captures the complex nonlinear ways one needs to think about children, play, and development. It is also a stimulating read for experienced psychoanalysts interested in the innovative conceptual integration of theory and in expanding their understanding of the potential of play and playfulness for development and therapeutic action.

This rich, dense, and ambitious book stands on its own as a comprehensive psychotherapy primer on working with younger children. The authors note that this volume can serve as a companion to their previous book, Normal Child and Adolescent Development: A Psychodynamic

The importance of theory in the life of a practicing psychoanalyst is obvious to anyone who has navigated psychoanalytic training or studied the dynamics of analytic institutes. In the United States most candidates are trained in institutes where a dominant theoretical position can be identified even when there is an attempt to teach comparative theories. No matter how much effort is made to ensure that candidates are exposed to all the major competing theories, most institutes are dedicated to preserving an approach to theory that favors a particular school. The conviction that one theory is more successful than another appears to develop despite the fact that no studies have proven the superiority of any particular theoretical school. Different theories have distinct views of psychological development and its failures that in turn result in radically different approaches to psychoanalytic treatment. It is clear that training in an institute oriented toward classical ego psychology, contemporary conflict theory, self psychology, or Kleinian, Bionian, or relational theory is likely to result in analysts who follow that theory in organizing their clinical work.

Despite the partisan nature of competing theoretical schools of psychoanalysis, there has been a tendency among some psychoanalysts to adopt a position that allows the use of multiple theories, shifting from one perspective to another, depending on aspects of the chosen theory that suit a particular patient at a particular moment in the analysis. Some analysts applaud this blending of theories as a practical solution to competition in the marketplace of ideas; they report being able to apply seemingly incompatible theories, with beneficial results, to the analyses they conduct.

Psychoanalytic Approaches to the Treatment of Psychosis

Outpatient Treatment of Psychosis: Psychodynamic Approaches to Evidence-Based Practice. Edited by David L. Downing and Jon Mills. New York: Routledge, 2017, xxiv + 264 pp., $149.95 hardcover, $45.95 paperback.

Michael Garrett

Michael Garrett

45 Clinton Street
Lambertville, NJ 08530
E-mail: michael.garrett@downstate.edu

Michael Garrett   Professor of Psychiatry and Director of Psychotherapy Training, SUNY Downstate Medical Center; faculty, Psychoanalytic Association of New York.

Every generation of psychotherapists in the last century includes a group of clinicians dedicated to the psychotherapy of psychosis. The contributors to Outpatient Treatment of Psychosis can claim this ancestry. Although the multi-author chapters in the book all affirm the importance of psychotherapy in the treatment of psychosis, the collection reflects a variety of different perspectives, including the views of Freud, Klein, Bion, Winnicott, Jung, Lacan, and Spotnitz, among others. Readers will find different parts of the book new or familiar, depending on their personal theoretical background. Given the wonderful imprecision of language, because the same psychological idea can be expressed in different words, as I read this engaging book I asked myself, “Is this concept new to me, or is it a rephrasing of something I already know? If it is truly new, do I have sufficient background to understand what the writer is saying?”

David Downing, one of the volume's editors, sets the stage in chapter 1 by tracing the community mental health movement of the 1950s-1970s in the U.S. back to efforts to treat servicemen traumatized in World War II. The policy of “deinstitutionalization” ushered in by neuroleptic medication led to psychotic patients being discharged to the community, but the subsequent decline of liberal politics saw funding for community mental health dry up. Large numbers of chronically psychotic persons have now been reinstitutionalized in prisons and nursing homes. If the treatment of psychosis has withered in the public sector, Downing asks, what can be done on behalf of psychotic persons needing care?

Sarah Ackerman

Sarah Ackerman

53 South Main Street, Suite 311
Hanover, NH 03755
E-mail: ackerman@post.harvard.edu

Sarah Ackerman  

An autobiographical interpretation of latent meanings in Freud's Interpretation of Dreams is undertaken in order to explore oedipal themes in Freud's writing and their role in his resistance to writing the book. By looking at how the book performs its message, in presenting a collection of Freud's personal dreams that talk to each other and inspire meaning-making, we can see how readers are drawn into the role of interpreters/analysts. This process makes the oedipal myth palpable long before Freud mentions it. It also illustrates an analytic process that analysts are all required to follow. Finally, the process reveals something in Freud that could be apprehended only after his ideas were conveyed—the unending process of interpretation, in which every conclusion gives rise to new ideas, inspiring new conclusions.

Matthew Steinfeld

Matthew Steinfeld

1 Long Wharf Drive, SATU, Box 30
New Haven, CT 06511

Matthew Steinfeld   Assistant Professor of Psychiatry, Yale School of Medicine; Associate Director of Clinical Training, Substance Abuse Treatment Unit, Connecticut Mental Health Center; psychoanalytic candidate, NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis; board member, Psychoanalysis for Social Responsibility (Section IX, Division 39, American Psychological Association).

“History affects us whether we know it or not.” So begins Molly Castelloe's timely film Vamik's Room, a documentary about the life and work of Vamik D. Volkan, M.D.1 Linking different moments of Volkan's personal history to his clinical work and social activism, Castelloe introduces us to Volkan's Turkish Cypriot roots, his early training as a psychiatrist-psychoanalyst, and his pioneering use of clinical psychoanalytic concepts in attempting to understand social conflicts and international relations.

Civil unrest and war on the island of Cyprus became a catalyst for Volkan's lifelong attempt to understand and treat political maladies that seem to be symptomatic of the nation-state. The documentary walks the viewer through many of its subject's central theoretical ideas and ethnographic observations, which are certainly worthy of engaging and reexamining during this period of global unrest, regression toward ahistorical pseudo-nativism, and a frightening trend in which authoritarian forms of governance are making gains in countries around the globe. Volkan's observations on these topics suggest a common political disorder with varied symptomatic manifestations depending on the cultures, religions, languages, and histories of the polities involved.

The propensity for intergroup violence, Volkan begins by saying, has everything to do with “large group identity” and the need to protect it.

Marilia Aisenstein

Marilia Aisenstein

243 Boulevard Raspail
75014 Paris
FRANCE
E-mail: marilia.aisenstein@gmail.com

Marilia Aisenstein  

I am pleased and honored to have been asked to discuss this excellent article by Kathryn Zerbe. Its theme, announced at the outset, involves two notions: that of the secret and that of somatic disorganization in the patient and somatic signals affecting the psychoanalyst through the countertransference. As a French psychoanalyst and psychosomatician of the Paris School of Psychosomatics, I am particularly sensitive to the concept of “embodied countertransference” used, or perhaps created, by Zerbe. Like her, I believe that countertransference concerns the body of the psychoanalyst, who must let him- or herself be guided by somatic feelings.

I must say at the outset that the clinical examples in the article are remarkable. In addition, the review of the psychoanalytic literature is thorough and well-documented.

I would add here only a text by Pierre Marty (1952), which I translated and commented on in IJP under the title “The Narcissistic Difficulties Presented to the Observer by the Psychosomatic Problem.” This strictly theoretical text comprises Marty's observations and reflections on the psychosomatician's countertransference toward the somatic patient, even in theory.

Due to his illness, according to Marty, the somatic patient has a fragmented representation of his body, as well as a tendency to suppress the quality of the otherness of the object he is relating to. These particularities of the patient's mental functioning result in a modification of the psychoanalyst/psychosomatician's countertransference.

Old and Dirty Gods: Religion, Anti-Semitism, and the Origins of Psychoanalysis. By Pamela Cooper-White. New York: Routledge, 2018, x + 294 pp., $145.00 hardcover, $46.95 paperback.

Arnold Richards

Arnold Richards

200 East 89th Street, Apt. 45B
New York, NY 10128

Arnold Richards   Former Editor, Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association; Training and Supervising Analyst, New York Psychoanalytic Institute.

Pamela Cooper-White, the author of this well-researched and illuminating volume, is highly qualified to deal with her subject. Her background, interests, and training have prepared her well for writing what I believe is a landmark volume on psychoanalysis, religion, and anti-Semitism.

She is the Christiane Brooks Johnson Professor of Psychology and Religion at Union Theological Seminary in New York. An ordained priest in the Episcopal Church, she conducted the research for this volume as the Fulbright Freud Visiting Scholar in Vienna from 2013 to 2014. Her two doctorates are from Harvard University and the Institute for Clinical Social Work in Chicago (the latter a psychoanalytic, clinical, and research degree). Her interests and scholarship are wide-ranging: pastoral theology, postmodern psychoanalytic theory and practice, and intersubjectivity.

Cooper-White offers the reader two theses: First, that the views of Freud's Wednesday Night Circle were more complex and conformed less strictly to Freud's ideas than has often been assumed There was an “orthodox” tone set by Freud, a commitment to “established truth and the principles confirmed by our scientific method” (p. 32), but also a tolerance of deviations. The second thesis, unanticipated by the author, is that psychoanalysis, early on, was shaped by the anti-Semitism of the surrounding culture; “one of the most pervasive—as well as sometimes denied—social forces in twentieth century Vienna,” it inevitably suffused the thinking of Freud's circle, both consciously and unconsciously (p. 7).

Cooper-White's primary sources are (1) the minutes of Freud's Wednesday night group, which began in 1902, kept by Otto Rank from 1906 until his departure from Vienna in 1915 for military duty during World War I; and (2) the writings of psychoanalysts on the topic of religion in the journal Imago (founded in 1912) and other publications.

Creativity and Perversion: Waiting for the Muse

Graeme Taylor

Graeme Taylor

130 Carlton Street, Suite 1406
Toronto, ON M5A 4K3
CANADA
E-mail: graeme.taylor@utoronto.ca

Graeme Taylor  

creativity, perversion, macrophilia, trauma, aggression, castration anxiety, annihilation anxiety

Although there is an extensive psychoanalytic literature on perversion, and numerous articles about creativity, few authors have explored relations between creativity and perversion. In particular, the role of childhood trauma and its impact on object relations has not been examined in patients with perversions whose creativity is blocked. In association with preoedipal anxieties and fantasies, childhood trauma can not only contribute to the development of perversion, but can also inhibit or distort the creative process by establishing an inner world characterized by the presence of a threatening internal bad object and the elusiveness of an internal good object. Though it is essential to help these patients establish an identification with the phallic father, an internal good maternal object, in the form of a muse, needs to be retrieved to bring inspiration and reduce the anxieties generated by an internal bad object, thereby facilitating the pursuit of authentic creative work. A detailed case report illustrates how this theoretical perspective guided the treatment approach to a male patient with macrophilia who was struggling to realize his creative potential.

Introduction

Jonathan House

Jonathan House

1050 Fifth Avenue, #4
New York, NY 10028
E-mail: jonathan.house@gmail.com

Jonathan House  

Psychoanalysis has an originary tendency, a preoccupation with origins, a vocation for origins—at the beginning it was the origins of symptoms, then came dreams, then slips of the tongue and then jokes, and from early on to the present, the origin of sexuality, of desire. The paper presented here was written in 1981 and, although there are anticipations of Laplanche's major theorizing of sexuality, what he calls the General Theory of Seduction (hereafter GTS), the essay does not emphasize the problematic of the origin of sexuality. This is partly because the GTS was first presented in 1987, six years after this essay was presented at the Faculté de Psychologie de l'UNAM (Mexico). The essay addresses central themes in Klein's theorizing and technique but does not touch on Klein's approach to sexuality, at least not explicitly. Laplanche's essay speaks for itself, but JAPA has asked that I situate it within the development of Laplanche's thinking and clarify a few phrases that may be obscure.

Laplanche insists that the unconscious, the repressed sexual unconscious, is the proper object of psychoanalysis. As only humans have such an unconscious, psychoanalysis is concerned with the origin and nature of the human subject. In other words, psychoanalysis is an anthropology in Kant's sense of the term. Laplanche's writing emphasizes the problematic of origins. In the 1950s he focused on the nature of the unconscious and by the 1960s he focused on what for him was almost the same thing: the nature and origin of human sexuality, sexuality in the expanded sense that Freud gave it. Laplanche considered the General Theory of Seduction his most important contribution. He first presented the theory in New Foundations for Psychoanalysis (1987, pp. 101-174) and called it “the hinge of my work” (personal communication). In 2002 he called the GTS The Fundamental Anthropological Situation (2007, pp. 99-114).

Commentary on Laplanche

Richard Simpson

Richard Simpson

1064 Avenue Bernard, Suite 50
Montreal, Quebec H2V 1V2
CANADA
E-mail: richardbsimpson@me.com

Richard Simpson  

Laplanche's approach to theory focuses on what makes psychoanalysis the discipline it is: the unconscious, whose effects are felt by the analyst not only as clinician but equally as theorist. I will focus in this commentary on Laplanche's method of critiquing psychoanalytic theory. One has the impression that Laplanche was a practitioner of the principle of Occam's razor, the philosophical precept that in problem solving “entities are not to be multiplied without necessity.” In the context of psychoanalysis this means that when one is presented with competing hypotheses based on the same clinical observations, one should select the solution with the fewest assumptions. We see clearly how Laplanche eliminates redundant theorizing in his critique of Klein's “pairs.”

Notwithstanding the rhetorical flourish of asking whether Klein is a witch needing to be burned at the stake, Laplanche subjects her to the same mode of critique he uses when “attacking,” as he puts it, Freud or, for that matter, his daughter Anna or Lacan. Moreover, Klein may be in for special treatment only to the extent that she took certain aspects of Freud's theorizing (phylogenetic inherited instinctual sexual fantasies and the death drive) and then pushed them even further. These aspects of Freud's thinking receive Laplanche's most severe criticism.

I am writing here from my point of view as someone who has been interested in French psychoanalysis over the years and written about Lacan, Michel de M'Uzan (Simpson 2017), and more recently Laplanche himself (Simpson in press). Laplanche was an analysand of Lacan but managed to not become a Lacanian. If there is a transmission from Lacan to Laplanche, one might say that it has taken the form of a “return to Freud” well beyond Lacan's vision of “return.” Laplanche's warrant for his critique of theory is his profound study of Freud's German text,

From the Editors

Anne Adelman and Jennifer Stuart

Jennifer Stuart

E-mail: anne.adelmanphd@gmail.com

jennifer.stuart.phd@gmail.com

Jennifer Stuart  

Personal Encounters in Psychoanalysis

In this section we encounter personal reflections from analysts about their analytic selves. We all have yearned for a glimpse of the inner workings of the mind of the analyst, both within and beyond the walls of the consulting room. How do analysts determine what to say, what to interpret, when to wait tactfully, when to sally forth? How do they cope with their own feelings of loneliness, disappointment, pleasure, anger, and pain? In her review of Robert Winer and Kerry Malawista's Who's Behind the Couch, a collection of personal and often intimate interviews—of psychoanalysts, by psychoanalysts, from around the globe—Aisha Abbasi shares her marvel and delight in being able to engage, as a reader, in a compelling and thought-provoking set of conversations. In many of the interviews, Abbasi discovers gems that illuminate something in a new way or bring important questions to mind. Her review brings the reader into dialogue with the analysts interviewed and with the editors who assembled the book. In these pages, Abbasi tells us, we will be able to discover the analyst behind the couch: “The interviews collected here provide inspiring insights into how and why these analysts became the analysts they are today, and how they help their patients.” The conversations in this book are fascinating, engaging, and an important pathway to understanding—as Winer and Malawista suggest in their title—“the heart and mind of the psychoanalyst.”

Sybil Houlding's review of Stephanie Brody's Entering Night Country: Psychoanalytic Reflections on Loss and Resilience offers a different view of the analyst's internal experience, this one through the lens of loss and the transience of life. Houlding offers a close reading of Brody's intimate reflection on loss, mortality, and the art of psychoanalysis. In her volume, Brody explores the power of analytic work to create boundaries within which patient and analyst can risk a deep exploration of intimacy.
