Psychoanalysis and the Divine

Silent Virtues: Patience, Curiosity, Privacy, Intimacy, Humility, and Dignity. By Salman Akhtar. New York: Routledge, 2019, x + 180 pp., $37.95 paperback.

Paula Hamm LPC PC

Paula Hamm

6723 Whittier Avenue, Suite 405D
McLean, VA 22101
E-mail: paulajhamm@gmail.com

Paula Hamm   Faculty, Washington Baltimore Center for Psychoanalysis; Chair, Psychoanalysis / Religion and Spirituality Committee, American Psychoanalytic Association.

We value virtue but do not discuss it. The honest book keeper, the faithful wife, the earnest scholar get little of our attention compared to the embezzler, the tramp or the cheat.

—John Steinbeck (1962, p. 134)

The heart of humanity is our endless desire to make sense of our own being. Psychoanalysis was founded on alleviating the suffering that comes with being human. We all agree on that. Suffering drives people into our clinical offices and moves people to fall on their knees, as pictured on the cover of Salman Akhtar's new book, Silent Virtues. Akhtar reveals, “I have found promptings to renounce a well-chiseled selfhood alluring as well as frightening. I have flirted with desires to learn more about Buddhism and Sufism, to visit sacred places of the world, and to retreat into non-worldly solitude but found such inner callings a bit scary” (p. 102).

Courageously, Akhtar has been venturing into the underpinnings of spirituality and religion by addressing human attributes such as forgiveness (2002), goodness (2009a,b), gratitude (2013a), generosity (2013b), patience (2015a), dignity (2015b), and humility (2018); his inner emotional biddings crisscross into the field of religion and spirituality. This new book, Silent Virtues, continues to consider what Hildegard von Bingen (1098-1179) proclaimed, “Divinity is aimed at humanity.”

One of psychoanalysis's greatest paradoxes is that the field was founded on being open to the unknown, the unconscious. When it comes to the notions of religion and spirituality, however, historically these have tended to be viewed by analysts through the lens of psychopathology.

Christian Seulin

Christian Seulin

10 rue Renan
69007 Lyon
FRANCE

Christian Seulin   Training and Supervising Analyst, Paris Psychoanalytical Society (SPP); Director, Lyon Institute, SPP.

After Guérir du mal d'aimer (Recovering from the Pain of Loving) in 1998 and Avant d'être celui qui parle (Before Being Someone Who Speaks) in 2006, Jean-Claude Rolland now invites us to join him on another rich and difficult psychoanalytic journey. The path we follow, while constantly referring to Freud, crosses those of other renowned creators, artists and poets, who are called upon to illustrate—along with clinical material from patients—the singular way of “seeing” that animates psychoanalytic treatment, with what Rolland calls “the mind's eye.”

The title of the book refers to Shakespeare's Hamlet, where in act 1 scene 2, the eponymous hero says, “In my mind's eye, Horatio.” Jean-Claude Rolland borrows this phrase to describe the sudden emergence in the transference of images and words that come from the past. Thus he draws a parallel between psychoanalytic treatment and artistic creation. According to Rolland, what the two have in common is that they both “set free the Titans of childhood from the chains that reduce them to silence and obscurity and, in so doing, free the individual from ignorance of his or her origins” (p. 13; all translations mine).

Rolland proposes that an image, visible or invisible, lies somewhere between language and the negativity of the unconscious and stands in contrast to them. The interplay between image and language can be detected in the treatment process. Dreams are where images and language are woven together. The language of dreams can be at once a primitive language that is decorative, a secret language to be interpreted, and an aesthetic language of love that should be heard and shared. Rolland writes that “the dream talks to the object of which it speaks” (p. 50), and that analysts must “see themselves in what they are hearing” (p. 54).

Primitive language readily becomes action, giving way to drive impulses; the language of logos, by contrast, brings into play an element of renunciation. The two forms of language are combined through metamorphoses, transformations created by the mind. Throughout his work, Rolland highlights the tension between primitive language and logos.

Kerry Malawista   Training and Supervising Analyst, Contemporary Freudian Society; Co-chair, New Directions in Writing, Washington Center for Psychoanalysis.

Learning along the Way: Further Reflections on Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy, by Patrick Casement, is a collection of his previously published papers, presentations, and interviews. He offers them as

1102

individual chapters, which he annotates with the context in which each was written. In his introduction, Casement makes it clear that he is at the end of his life, that he has closed his psychoanalyst practice, and that, after a bout of near-fatal cancer, this will be his final book.

This volume, then, is the body of Casement's work, his greatest hits. It is suffused with integrity, wisdom, and a passionate commitment to the work of analysis, supervision, and training. His theories are accessible to the student of psychoanalysis, yet wise and rewarding for the seasoned analyst. Consistent throughout his work, including his first book, Learning from the Patient (1985), he reminds us that to learn from our patients we need to come from a place of uncertainty, remain open to what they can teach us, and keep our preconceived notions and theoretical viewpoints at bay. He maintains that no real change can happen when ideas are imparted from an all-knowing analyst; rather, it is the slow discovery by the patient that invites change.

Central to his work is the idea that patients must be allowed the freedom to be themselves, and that therapists must learn the language their patients are speaking without imposing their own. He encourages analysts/therapists to sit patiently with their questions and to allow understanding to unfold. He elaborates on early themes in his writing: trial identification (when we sit with a patient, we should think about how they will hear what we say); the otherness of the other (while we are encouraged to put ourselves in another's shoes, we should keep in mind that like-minded is not equivalent to sameness), and the value of uncertainty and communication in treatment. He outlines the ways we are at risk of creating “imprisoned minds”; instead of helping our patients and supervisees think for themselves, there is pressure for them to give up their thoughts to accommodate the analyst's line of thinking. To avoid this imprisonment, he returns throughout the book to the concept of internal supervision, and the importance for the analyst of self-monitoring the interactions between herself and the patient (or supervisee).

Commentary on Saketopoulou

Jonathan House

Jonathan House

1050 Fifth Avenue
New York, NY 10028
E-mail: jonathan.house@gmail.com

Jonathan House  

Psychoanalysts seem particularly vulnerable to the temptation to create basic concepts. The result is a confusion of conceptual tongues. Some papers use only a few concepts but don't notice that they are mutually contradictory. In other papers the concepts are so numerous and juggled so quickly that it is hard to notice when one or two drop out of sight. As Paul Gilroy has remarked, “We now appear to have reached the point where theories and concepts are almost fashion items with the shelf life of cheap tee-shirts” (quoted in Dawes 2011).

Generosity is a virtue and theoretical eclecticism a convenience, but neither generosity nor eclecticism should be used to cover up conceptual problems. On this matter, Laplanche (2007) hits the right note: he calls his essay on the conceptual distinction between drive and instinct

a clarification, which, in our discipline, is primarily a catharsis—something for which psychoanalysis has a powerful and continuing need. Robert Stoller, with his great freedom of thought and in his own occasionally very amusing manner, compares current psychoanalytic theory to the Pantheon of imperial Rome, where coexisted the temples of Isis and of Jupiter, a few early Christian churches, the temples of Mithra, and so on. In psychoanalysis too we add little temples, private mansions, and supplementary shrines onto the Freudian forum, without worrying about their coherence. A pinch of the symbolic, a dash of leaning-on, a knob of the negative, a small measure of seduction, a sprig of transitivity—all without worrying ourselves about what it is we are building upon or how these cohere with it [Laplanche 2007, p. 5].

I believe Avgi Saketopoulou's “overwhelm” is one of those rare novel concepts that should be retained. For this reason alone, overwhelm deserves careful examination. These comments aim to establish the metapsychological foundation for overwhelm on Freud's and Laplanche's theorizing of binding.

Training and Supervising Analyst, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research; Lecturer, Institute for Comparative Literature and Society.

Ghosted and Ancestral Selves in Hamlet: Loewald's “Present Life” and Winnicott'S “Potential Space” in Shakespeare's Play

Gavriel Reisner

Gavriel Reisner

55 West End Avenue, Apt. S15I
New York, NY 10023
E-mail: gavriel.reisner@gmail.com

Gavriel Reisner  

ghosts, ancestors, father-ghost, space, time, friend as good object, ego, identification, internalization, object relations, transference, transitional space

This psychoanalytic reading of Hamlet places Shakespeare's play in the theoretical contexts of Loewald on time and Winnicott on space. For Loewald the subject moves from past to present, in a therapeutic fashion, through the intervention of the analyst, a contemporary object. A redemption of time occurs in the internalized action of thought and dialogue. In Winnicott the redemptive movement is from an internal-subjective to an external-objective way of perceiving. The passage occurs in a transitional space where the presence of another allows the discovery of a world. Hamlet suffers from a ghosted self emptied in submission to the father-ghost. In the temporality of thought and the spatiality of action Hamlet moves toward an ancestral self, filled and stable, through the mediation of Horatio, his friend-counselor-analyst.

Shock Therapy: Psychology, Precarity, and Well-Being in Postsocialist Russia. By Tomas Matza. Durham: Duke University Press, 2018, xxii + 306 pp., $104.95 hardcover, $27.95 paperback.

Philip Pomper

Philip Pomper

1165 Park Avenue South, Apt. 10D
New York, NY 10128
E-mail: ppomper@wesleyan.edu

Philip Pomper   William F. Armstrong Professor of History, Emeritus, Wesleyan University.

In his study of what he calls “the psychotherapeutic turn” in St. Petersburg, Russia, the anthropologist Tomas Matza works from a Foucauldian point of view with a dash of Marx thrown in. The importance of class shows in Foucault's critique of “bourgeois social difference,” but by shifting his

375

examination of the technologies of power to biopolitics rather than class conflict as such, Foucault refocused Marxian class analysis and macro-predictions to the micro-level of power. Matza practices his critical anthropology at the sites where psychotherapists acquire their skills, interact with their peers, deliver their therapeutic services, and experience their own enthusiasms, dilemmas, and conflicts.

Matza's terminology reflects his complex position. “Neoliberal” has negative connotations rather than positive ones. It signifies the rule of capitalism and the embourgeoisement of professionals in the health sector. “Precarity” denotes precariousness of care. “The psychotherapeutic turn is not simply a symptom, or function, of neoliberal governmentality. Instead it has inaugurated a form of precarious care that intersects with some, but not all, of the forms of rule specific to neoliberal states” (p. 229). Matza thus deploys his critical anthropology at the level of biopolitics: the loci of power, the damage done by experts, and the ethical quandaries caregivers face. His subjects left a world that had discouraged uniqueness and the self as opposed to the collective, but that now approved of individual emotional needs and the quest for happiness.

Matza's study of postsocialist Russia focuses on the period 2005-2013, when he did fieldwork in the Russian Federation. He had imagined himself doing an anthropological study of emerging psychological therapies serving a new Russian individual, with both therapist and client reflecting the breakdown of Soviet orthodoxies and also the therapeutic eclecticism of that period in the West. In 2005 many American academics still embraced radical attitudes of the nineties imported from France, but by the first decade of the twenty-first century issues of race and gender had eclipsed the postmodern moment. Matza, however, still relies on Foucault and Gilles Deleuze for his exposé of the harm done by experts. To be sure, he is quite eclectic. He uses Marxian ideas (for example, the notion of “false consciousness”) but shares postmodern assumptions critical of nineteenth- and twentieth-century utopias based on scientific worldviews. Foucault's postmodernism added a micro-dimension missing in the Marxian and positivistic worldviews, and also appreciation for subjectivity and individual emotional needs above and beyond a critique of bourgeois society. Every aspect of society and culture, including psychological symptoms and the therapies designed to treat them, reflects Matza's belief in the domination of Western capitalism and Russian biopolitics.

This book's title sounds evocative echoes: In “The Circus Animals' Desertion,” a late work, W. B. Yeats located poetry's humble origins in “the foul rag and bone shop of the heart.” Early in “The Waste Land,” T. S. Eliot censured April as “the cruelest month,” bringing “spring rain,” nature's reawakening, and thereby stirring “memory and desire.” Analysts will recognize too another echo, Bion's advice to listen without “memory or desire,” to enable both patient and analyst to be fully present in the moment.

San José

Eddy Carrillo   Member, Asociación de Psicoanálisis Socio-Crítico; Director, Centro de Estudio Psicoanalíticos; Professor of Psychology, Universidad de Costa Rica and Universidad Autónoma de Centro América.

Rafael Paz has been among the most influential psychoanalysts in Latin America over the last three decades. He was a founding member of the

919

Argentinian Society for Psychoanalysis after the period of crisis in his native country in the early 1970s (see Arbiser 2003). Since publication of his classic book on psychodynamic psychopathology (Paz 1976), he has been a central figure in both the training of analysts and the advancement of psychoanalytic theory and practice in Argentina.

Most people in Latin America interested in psychoanalysis and clinical psychology will be familiar with Paz's text on psychopathology. Contested Issues, however, is a different animal entirely. It is not so much read as it is experienced, and reading it is a complex and difficult experience to say the least. The reason for this is twofold: on one hand there is the form, the style or prose of the author; on the other hand, there is the content. Let us start with the former.

The first thing the reader faces when engaging with Contested Issues is that the book is written entirely in paragraphs containing a single sentence. The only exceptions are quotes by other authors—in a couple of cases Freud, in another Bion. This generates a reading experience that is disjointed, jumpy, and—until the reader gets accustomed to it—at times disorienting. A few chapters into the experience it becomes clear that the content of the author's ideas has influenced the form of his writing. Paz is delving into areas of the mind that have to do with what is disjointed, split, unrepresented, or poorly mentalized, or what might be considered primitive or preoedipal. It seems only natural, then, that his writing style should reflect these topics. There may be an additional reason for the form of the author's writing, which has both scientific and literary qualities; as Paz proposes, “we must fully accept that it is poetic reason which is pertinent to psychoanalysis” (p. 35; all translations mine).

It is important to mention that this book does not attempt to provide complete answers, be they technical or theoretical. It seems more intent on providing latitudes and longitudes, the lines and borders within which psychoanalysis takes place—the dynamic field, if you will, in which we practice. In that it is as much about raising questions as it is about proposing answers, Paz's book has parallels with the writings of Winnicott.

A Childhood Dream Fulfilled

Arnold Richards

Arnold Richards

200 East 89th Street
New York, NY 10128
E-mail: arniedr15@gmail.com

Arnold Richards  

Books made up my world from a very early age. While growing up, being a reader was inseparable from being myself. I wanted to be a writer from as far back as I can remember. In childhood and youth, I spent hours absorbed in reading and study at the Brooklyn Public Library in Grand Army Plaza. My earliest literary memory is reading in the Yiddish Forward, at the age of five, about the death of Sigmund Freud. I can even trace the beginning of my interest in becoming a psychoanalyst to that time.

I have often thought about the role of identification in my love of reading and my dreams of writing. In our core development we see the ways of being a self reflected in people we admire. My father's influence on me and my identification with him were both literary.1 As a librarian in a unit of Trotsky's army during the Russian revolution, he was in charge of a cart carrying books to the soldiers, though it pained him that in those days most of the soldiers were unable to read. He loved Russian literature—Gogol, Pushkin, Turgenev, Tolstoy. And he hoped I would read the great Russians in the original. Though he did not succeed in teaching me Russian, he did convey his love of literature to me, and I learned to read and write in Yiddish as well as English. Being bilingual increased my sensitivity to the nuances of language and eventually served me well as a writer.

Among the great authors who were my childhood heroes was the Yiddish writer Isaac Metzger. I read his children's books in Yiddish. Metzger became known as editor of the Bintel Brief, a section in the Forward (the Yiddish newspaper) of readers' letters and stories of their

1In 1988, as a memorial to my father, I sponsored the exhibit Revolutions in Print: Jewish Publishing Under the Czars and the Soviets, at the Yivo Institute for Jewish Research. It examined the changing role of the printed word in Jewish life in Russia and the Soviet Union.

Former Editor, Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association.

A version of this essay was published as “My Literary Childhood Dream Fulfilled” in Clio's Psyche 26(1): 102-105.

JAPA Prize Announcements

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association

JAPA Prize

The $1000 JAPA Prize is awarded to the author of the best paper published in the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association during the previous year. All publications, including review articles, research articles, essays and book essays, may be considered for the award. Candidates for the award are nominated by the members of the JAPA Editorial Board, with the final decision being made by the Editor-in-Chief.

JAPA New Author Prize

JAPA's $500 New Author Prize recognizes significant contributions to the journal by new authors. It is awarded for the best published manuscript by a new author, and is presented at the Winter Meetings of the American Psychoanalytic Association.

A new author is considered to be someone who has not previously published in JAPA or any other major psychoanalytic journal and whose psychoanalytic career is in its early stages. A mentoring/editorial group has been established to review submissions by new authors and assist in developing manuscripts for publication in JAPA.

Manuscripts must be submitted via the JAPA submission process, using the online system at http://mc.manuscriptcentral.com/japa. At the time of submission, authors should indicate in the cover letter that the paper is to be considered for the New Author Prize, and may ask that it be directed to the mentoring/editorial group for assistance in manuscript development. Final decision about publication remains with the Editorial Board and the Editor-in-Chief, as does selection of the annual prize-winning paper.

Intimacy and Separateness in Psychoanalysis. By Warren S. Poland. New York: Routledge, 2018, xxx + 205 pp., $145.00 hardcover, $36.95 paperback.

Charles Hanly

Charles Hanly

106 Elm Avenue
Toronto, ON M4W 1P2
CANADA
E-mail: charlesmhanly@gmail.com

Charles Hanly   Training and Supervising Analyst, Toronto Institute of Psychoanalysis; Professor Emeritus (philosophy), University of Toronto; Past President, International Psychoanalytical Association.

When invited to review a highly praiseworthy book, the obvious and economical thing to do is simply praise it. Warren Poland's Intimacy and Separateness in Psychoanalysis is a work of psychoanalytic vitality, depth of thought, and humanity expressed with subtlety and clarity.

Its very title tells a tale in its hint of paradox. Paradoxes raise questions and stimulate thought. Inevitably analysts must come to know and understand sympathetically the most intimate details of trauma in their patients' libidinal and aggressive lives, from childhood to maturity, in order to help them resolve their symptoms and blossom in their lives, all the while keeping separate the stream of their own sexual and aggressive needs and wants for satisfaction elsewhere. Intimacy and separateness are fundamental determinants of adequate psychoanalytic work. When they fail, so does analysis. They are also the stuff of life and tragedy. The tragic flaw of Shakespeare's King Lear is his inability to tolerate Cordelia's youthful sexual love for another man. Given the repetition of fixated sexual love and aggression in the maternal, paternal, and sibling transference relations of the analytic situation, an obligation is placed on the analyst to avoid Lear's flaw of appearing to give while unconsciously seeking to possess. This potentially tragic paradox in life and psychoanalysis is explored by Poland from diverse perspectives. If intimacy is required, how is separateness possible? If separateness is required, how is intimacy possible? If both are required by psychoanalysis, is not our profession even more seriously impossible than Freud intended to say when he compared it with teaching and governing? Does not psychoanalysis impose logically contradictory and psychologically conflicting demands on us?

To the tasks imposed by this core issue in psychoanalysis, Poland brings a revealing humanistic attitude. The titles of the sections and chapters convey a playful irony—as in “Opening Conclusions.” It is an irony that banishes mediocre sincerity from the thoughtful consideration of a series of central issues in clinical psychoanalysis and in life, as in “Endings in Poetry, Psychoanalysis, and Life.

Harvey Schwartz   Training and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Association of New York (PANY) and the Psychoanalytic Center of Philadelphia.

Heneini, Abraham dutifully responds to the voice of his God. “I am here” to do your bidding and kill my son.

Three biblical generations later Joseph similarly declares to his father Heneini—I am here. This time, though, the presence of God is interiorized. There are no external voices of a deity, and Joseph's generativity is internally derived. He is the first biblical character to have the capacity to mourn. He and his father Jacob are the first father-son pair to weep together. Nathan Szajnberg's book explores the implications of this evolution from intergenerational violence to meaningful emotional connection between father and son.

In her most recent work, Helen Gediman has given us an account of more than fifty years in the evolution of her thought. An appropriate subtitle for the work would have been Inside the Mind of Helen Gediman. Her thinking over many years of psychoanalytic practice, teaching, and theorizing shows us her originality and flexibility of thought. In fact, her thinking shows several of the characteristics of the creative mind that I have described: “The ability to take the difficult path when there is a less anxiety provoking alternative and to ‘own’ one's work rather than view oneself as a passive receiver of ideas” and “The capacity to tolerate and maintain ambiguity and unclosure” (Nass 1993, p. 28).

Gediman has been able to allow contradictory points of view to coexist in her mind (and her writing), and to find imaginative ways to explain what would ordinarily seem a dilemma. The book takes the reader on a very personal journey into her evolution as a psychoanalyst, through her student days, through graduate school and analytic training, and to her current work and her manner of thinking as an analyst. She is forthcoming in sharing her thinking and dogged in maintaining her position in the face of criticism.

The book's introduction, by Fred Pine, offers a positive yet critical review of the work and sets forth Gediman's ability to hold and deal with points of view in conflict with each other.

Rodrigo Barahona

Rodrigo Barahona

1622A Beacon Street, Suite 301
Brookline, MA 02446

Rodrigo Barahona   Faculty, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; adjunct faculty, Center for Psychoanalytic Studies, San José, Costa Rica; Board Member, Boston Group for Psychoanalytic Studies; Associate Board Member, International Journal of Psychoanalysis.

Perhaps the most appropriate way to begin this review is with Rafael Paz's own words, succinctly, rather evocatively describing the “artisanal” (p. 11) function of psychoanalysis:

What do we aim for? Emotional opening, acceptance of the contradicting versions of ourselves, of the self, and of primary links, and a lessening of useless thinking and anguish. All in the service of subjective thickening, requiring crossing through resistances and accepting the rules of the transferential game, where, as any renewed metapsychology would recognize, passion and sense converge [p. 16; translation mine throughout].

The aptly titled Psychoanalyzing, by one of Argentina's leading contemporary analytic thinkers, is a work of profound psychoanalytic reflection. In it, Paz accomplishes a mix between delicate theoretical elaboration and musings or “systematic digressions” on analysis and contemporary life. By including this latter dimension, Paz joins the tradition of Latin American thinkers like Silvia Bleichmar and Pichon Riviere, who problematize the role of culture in psychoanalysis and view analysis as a method for the interrogation and opening up of new forms of subjectivation. He sees subjectivation as both limited and facilitated by societal and institutional values, ever expanding but also prone to forms of regression that lead to a deep feeling of discontent in society. Given where psychoanalysis and its subjects are now, what emerges from these pages is fresh, cutting-edge thinking.

Although each chapter is devoted to a separate topic, the author's central preoccupation is the nature of the psychoanalytic act, always linked tightly to his clinical work. The book has the feel of an analyst's diary, containing insights developed within the span of a few short pages, reminiscent of Bion's Cogitations (1992). One can almost imagine the author writing between sessions (in fact, in the first section there is a taste of that), as he reflects on what has just happened and as these thoughts connect with the hours of the previous days, weeks, and years.

Lacan on Psychosis: From Theory to Praxis, a volume edited by Jon Mills and David L. Downing, presents an excellent series of essays by experienced Lacanian analysts on psychotic phenomena. That being said, I found this book review more challenging to write than any of the dozens I have written. Since Lacan created a psychology unique to himself, albeit on the theme “A Return to Freud,” my difficulty involved the impossibility of refuting (or accepting) many of the statements made by the authors, especially in the first two chapters, and communicating my assessment of them to what I presume is a mostly non-Lacanian audience. In this regard, the Lacan apothegm “Language is meant to be misunderstood” rings all too true.

Jon Mills in his introduction and first chapter (“Lacan on Paranoiac Knowledge”) does a fine job of describing why Lacan has been rejected by mainstream schools of psychoanalysis and summarizing Lacan's three registers, or orders, of human existence—the Imaginary, the Symbolic, and the Real—to provide the non-Lacanian reader a summary of the foundations of Lacan's enterprise. Mills's brilliant first chapter elaborates Lacan's statement that “all knowledge is tinged with paranoia” and offers a dense, multifaceted discussion of this topic from a Lacanian perspective, a performance that will often leave the non-Lacanian analyst feeling lost. A student of Lacan for only the past ten years, I found Mills's discussion of paranoia to be like a pool of water that I enjoyed diving into and swimming around in; it presented fascinating and clinically useful ideas, most of them foreign to anything I have come across. But just as Lacan recommended “oracular” interpretations, I felt as if many of Mills's assertions are themselves oracular, requiring the reader to accept them solely on faith. For example, I could neither agree nor disagree with this one: “Unconscious fantasy systems largely serve a defensive function in the pre-oedipal child, fueling illusory misrecognitions as a way of fending off the aggressive violation of the imago's encroachment” (p. 24).

Patricia Gherovici  

Since the inception of psychoanalysis, Freud has been accused of being a “pansexualist.” In fact, the scandal caused by his sexual theories would be better explained by a more proper appellation: Freud was a “pan-bisexualist,” for bisexuality is a foundational psychoanalytic concept. It has far-reaching implications and yet is still felt to be ambiguous, contradictory, and full of impasses.

Considering that he had made great strides in his understanding of the riddle of sexuality, in a letter dated August 7, 1901, Freud announced that he was planning to write a book with the title Human Bisexuality, and asked his close friend and collaborator Wilhelm Fliess to be his coauthor: “The idea is yours. You remember my telling you years ago, when you were still a nose specialist and surgeon, that the solution lay in sexuality.” Freud added: “Several years ago you corrected me, saying that it lay in bisexuality—and I see you were right” (Masson 1985, p. 448). Indeed, Freud wanted to transform Fliess's ideas of a material, corporeal bisexuality, based on a comparison of nose bleeds and menstruation, into a universal psychoanalytic notion, freed from the constraints of biology.

The joint writing project never took place. Nonetheless, Frank Sulloway (1979) notes that the book on bisexuality that Freud wanted Fliess to join him in writing was actually published: “That seminal work eventually came out—minus Fliess's cooperation—under the better-known title Three Essays on the Theory of Sexuality” (p. 187). There Freud (1905) ascribed to bisexuality a foundational role in his theory of sexuality—it was of equal importance for both genders.

Co-founder and Director, Philadelphia Lacan Group; Associate Faculty, Psychoanalytic Studies Minor, University of Pennsylvania; Honorary Member, IPTAR: Founding Member, Das Unbehagen.

[[RunningHead]]

JAPA 67/1: The Psychoanalytic Body

Mitchell Wilson

Mitchell Wilson

Mitchell Wilson  

JAPA 67/1, our first issue of 2019, is dedicated to new writing on the psychoanalytic body. These essays and original papers all engage key questions regarding gender and sexuality, as well as aspects of embodied experience within psychoanalytic treatment. For some time now, JAPA has published papers pushing the boundaries of classical psychoanalytic theorizing on these matters and putting into question seemingly foundational binaries regarding gendered positions and sexual object choice (see, e.g., Saketopoulou 2014; Hansbury 2017). No doubt this publishing history redounds to our readers’ benefit, as we continue to receive creative and compelling submissions that fall under this broad category. Over the past year, as the essays and papers you are about to read went through our peer-review process, we gradually realized that we had a critical mass of outstanding work that, if brought together, would make for a thought-provoking and memorable JAPA issue. We wish to thank the authors for their efforts. We are also fortunate to have four commentators, Adrienne Harris, Patricia Gherovici, Jonathan House, and Marilia Aisenstein, whose outstanding contributions to psychoanalytic scholarship are well-known to many of our readers.

We begin 67/1 with Rosemary Balsam's essay, “On the Natal Body and Its Confusing Place in Mental Life.” As her title suggests, Balsam takes the reader through some key moments in the intellectual history of psychoanalytic theorizing on the body and describes its uncertain (i.e., “confused”) status in contemporary thinking about it. There is one fact, she asserts, about which there should be no confusion: what she calls “the programmed procreative potential of our natal bodies” (pp. 17-18): Why, she asks, might we not want to recognize this fact? Balsam offers a complex and penetrating answer to this question.

History of Countertransference: From Freud to the British Object Relations School. By Alberto Stefana. New York: Routledge, 2017, xii + 159 pp., $195.00 hardcover, $54.95 paperback.

Hilary Beattie

Hilary Beattie

15A East 10th Street, #1A
New York, NY 10003
E-mail: hjb1@cumc.columbia.edu

Hilary Beattie   Assistant Clinical Professor of Medical Psychology (in Psychiatry), Columbia University; faculty, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

In retrospect, the perils of the psychoanalytic method, or “talking cure,” for both its partners, should have been visible from the moment Josef Breuer fled in alarm from the erotic longings of “Anna O.” Yet it took another decade or so for Freud to identify the concept of “transference” in his treatment of “Dora” (1905), though he was still apparently unaware of the ways his own feelings and patriarchal, sexist assumptions (as well as his effective complicity in her parents' sexual intrigues) contributed to her abruptly breaking off the treatment. It took actual sexual transgression by a trusted analytic colleague to open Freud's eyes more fully to the risks inherent in the analytic situation. He casually coined the term “countertransference” in 1909, in a letter to Jung about the latter's emotional difficulties with his young Russian patient, Sabina Spielrein. At that moment Freud regarded such “experiences” as “necessary and hard to avoid,” although not leading to “lasting harm,” a conclusion he presumably modified when he soon learned from the patient herself of her sexual affair with Jung (p. 3).

From these beginnings the concept of countertransference (which Freud thought of as the result of the patient's influence on the analyst's unconscious feelings) has evolved and ramified in many complex directions, which Alberto Stefana has now set out to document more fully than anyone appears to have done before. He intends to use the concept as a lens through which to examine the “common ground” between the various analytic schools, amid their many theoretical and practical divergences.

[[RunningHead]]

The Wolf Man's Glückshaube: Rereading Sergei Pankejeff's Memoir

Anat Mahalel

Anat Mahalel

9 Haviva Reich Street
Haifa
ISRAEL
E-mail: anatmahalel@gmail.com

Anat Mahalel  

A comparative reading of Freud's canonical case study “From the History of an Infantile Neurosis” (1918) and the memoir written by the protagonist of that study, Sergei Pankejeff, known as the Wolf Man (1971a), centers on the complex matrix of meanings embodied in the act of lifting the veil. The neurotic symptom of a veil seemingly in front of the analysand's eyes is interpreted by Freud as a repetition of his birth in a Glückshaube (German for “caul,” literally a “lucky hood”). The veil is represented as an ambivalent object both for Freud and for Pankejeff, who are enticed by the sense of a final truth behind the veil yet constantly doubt the possibility of grasping it. For Freud, psychoanalysis is the very process of lifting the veil, yet his analysand remained for him an unsolved riddle. Pankejeff, in a volume dedicated to his identity as the Wolf Man (Gardiner 1971a), created an autobiographical text that deliberately avoids telling the story of the analysand, thus drawing a veil over his story. The paradox embodied in lifting the veil is discussed in relation to Walter Benjamin's distinction between materiality and truth and his notion of the inherent unity of the veil and the veiled (1925).

I am an avid reader of graphic novels. I love the simultaneous appearance of text and image on the page. The interplay of words and image is both visible and meaningful. Words—representing speech, thought, and narration—may appear in frames or little bubbles. They may run through or around an image; they may be absent. They may complement or contextualize the meanings conveyed by images, and vice versa. The meanings of image and word may be integrated or distinct. The story emerges through this confluence of forms, which are inextricable despite being discrete, and through an explicit juxtaposition of forms, each form serving as context for the other.

We read the words and images simultaneously and similarly, attending to their meanings. Reading images involves perceiving and thinking, just as reading words does. Yet we usually do not notice this thinking as we are reading; nor do we know the processes by which words and images achieve their effects.

[[RunningHead]]

A Note on Roy Schafer

Mitchell Wilson

Mitchell Wilson

E-mail: mdwmd57@icloud.com

Mitchell Wilson  

As time marches on, it seems to me that our institutional psychoanalytic memory regarding those who have touched us in enduring ways grows increasingly faulty. With the exception of a very few analysts around whom many of us organize our thinking and allegiances (Freud, Klein, Winnicott, Lacan, Bion), the truly gifted and more recently among us are too often forgotten. This should not be.

Here we pay tribute to the late Roy Schafer, a master of the psychoanalytic endeavor in all its facets. In addition to an obituary written by three analysts deeply impacted by Schafer as teacher, supervisor, and author, we reprint a seminal paper by Schafer, originally published in JAPA in 1974. As Gerald Fogel highlights in his generous introductory commentary, Schafer's “Problems in Freud's Psychology of Women” showcases an intelligence that comes to us only once or twice in a generation. In this paper Schafer takes on an entire edifice of theory and practice that was not only misguided, but was harmful to all concerned. We encounter in this paper, then, not only a mind of great agility, but also an ethical sensibility that demands the recurrent interrogation of received wisdom, especially “wisdom” that comes from the original source of all that comes after.

E-mail: mdwmd57@icloud.com

Anna Balas   Associate Professor of Psychiatry, Weill Cornell Medical College; faculty, New York Psychoanalytic Institute.

We are, these days, in the throes of the #MeToo movement, and we are simultaneously witnessing the separation of undocumented migrant children from their parents, right here in the United States. Worldwide, mass relocations, dislocations, and attendant misery have led to an epidemic increase of violence against women. This violence is an age-old problem in times of instability, crisis, and societal disruption, including the erosion of traditions and values protecting vulnerable women and children.

Whenever the subject involves external political reality, one needs to ask: What can psychoanalysts contribute that differs from the work of journalists and reporters? The situation is complex: (1) Pressure to be politically correct can polarize victims and victimizers. (2) In the interface between external and internal reality, political activism sometimes leads to self-righteousness. (3) Increasing political oppression, war, and poverty create situations of helplessness. How are we to use analytic principles to gain mastery?

Analysts can not only bear witness but can frame informed hypotheses about internal motivations. Analysts bring a more nuanced view, not simply dividing good and evil, victim and perpetrator. They recognize the importance of formative experiences and the consequences of early traumatization. They can formulate specific hypotheses about victim-perpetrator dynamics, repetition compulsion, and resiliency.

[[RunningHead]]

Editor's Note

Mitchell Wilson

Mitchell Wilson

E-mail: mdwmd57@icloud.com

Mitchell Wilson  

Jean Laplanche is one of a small handful of analysts whose creativity in the realm of psychoanalytic theory is matched by a rigorous and systematic reworking of basic Freudian concepts. Most of us in the English-speaking world first became aware of Laplanche's talent through the justly honored The Language of Psycho-Analysis (1967), a reference work coauthored with J.-B. Pontalis that remains, in my estimation, unmatched in the field. Over time, Laplanche's writings have become more familiar to us, in no small measure through the tireless efforts of Jonathan House. To House we owe a debt of gratitude for allowing JAPA to publish—for the first time in English—Laplanche's mischievously titled “Should We Burn Melanie Klein?” Laplanche's paper was given originally as a presentation in Mexico in 1981. Its title, though certainly jarring to some, in fact signals Laplanche's deep respect for Klein's intrepid push into the nether reaches of the psyche, as well as her willingness to populate this territory with a variety of figures (objects partial and whole), positions (paranoid-schizoid and depressive), and passions no less absolute than the instincts of Life and Death. But it is Klein's very fearlessness—or perhaps a presumptuousness that underwrites it—with which the admiring Laplanche struggles. As will become clear upon reading, Laplanche's critique engages fundamental questions regarding psychoanalytic theory and practice.

We have asked Jonathan House to write an introduction that contextualizes this paper within the larger corpus of Laplanche's writings. And we are fortunate to have illuminating commentaries by David Bell, Erik Gann, and Richard Simpson. We are all the grateful beneficiaries of their efforts.

This volume is the first of a projected series of three edited commentaries on the famous but seldom fully read Écrits of Jacques Lacan. He gave this title to thirty-four articles and lectures from various sources that were assembled as a book in 1966. Some were already well known to his followers; others were more obscure, all the more so as Lacan's prose was revealed as extremely difficult to decipher and its content replete with fragmentary references and allusions that would puzzle most readers, as he himself acknowledged. Muller and Richardson deserve credit for their pioneering guide to the Écrits published in 1982; that book introduced the work to a broader audience, although their scope was necessarily limited. Bruce Fink (2004) has published a reading of several of the essays. Although some had already been translated, the complete English version prepared by Fink did not appear until 2007. Of course, many discussions of Lacanian theory refer to parts of the Écrits. The current work, however, presents a unique attempt by scholars to place each of the articles in its historical and intellectual context, to make sense of the knotty prose as much as possible, and to offer interpretations of Lacan's intended meaning. I would judge the first two objectives eminently successful, while the third runs into the almost insurmountable obstacle of intentional ambiguity and linguistic playfulness that characterize most of Lacan's writings.

Commentary on Laplanche

Erik Gann

Erik Gann

3628 Sacramento Street, Suite A
San Francisco, CA 94118
E-mail: egannmd@gmail.com

Erik Gann  

What has once come to life clings tenaciously to its existence. One feels inclined to doubt sometimes whether the dragons of primaeval days are really extinct.

—Sigmund Freud (1937, p. 229)

The devil made me do it!

—Flip Wilson

To begin with, I would register a disagreement about the beginning, that is, about the English translation of the title of Laplanche's paper, “Faut-il brûler Melanie Klein?” My literal rendering of his language into English would be “Must One Burn Melanie Klein?” or “Does One Have to …?” or “Is It Necessary/Required to …?” It might be considered a small shift in meaning and nuance but is not an insignificant one at all, to my ear.1 “Should we” implies a consideration, a prospective choice based on some principle. “Must we” implies a desire to avoid or at least to reconsider a choice or an action.

The transposition in meaning indicates for me the alteration from a proposition to eliminate a peril to one rather of considering the prevention of such an elimination. In either case, Laplanche is playing with us; and the dialectic that he insists on our acknowledging throughout is already at work in his titular invocation. I shall return to this later in considering the immensely important position he articulates against mechanistic theoretical and clinical notions.

But for now it must simply be conceded that we, all of us, are possessed by things we cannot know and yet cannot dispel. Freud (1923)

1The French verb falloir means “to be necessary,” “to need to,” or “must.” It is impersonal, meaning that it has only one grammatical person: the third person singular.

Training and Supervising Analyst and Past President, San Francisco Center for Psychoanalysis; Principal, the Boswell Group.

The author thanks Phyllis Cath, Michael Levin, Mitchell Wilson, and Henry Smith for helpful suggestions for this commentary.

This book is an enthusiastic, wide-ranging tribute to Anni Bergman, “whose commitment to re-evaluating theory and practice from an integrative and developmental perspective, and pioneering work with Margaret Mahler, has directly influenced many of the contributors.” The broad range of topics attests Bergman's personal impact through teaching and through discussions she has led, both in psychoanalytic centers and on the borders of our field. That the contributors to the volume have been profoundly moved by her personal impact is reflected in the thirteen chapters, of which only five explicitly refer to Bergman's writings.

The first and last chapters are the most comprehensive and scholarly in the book. Harold Blum returns in the first chapter, “Emotional Development and Affects,” to a long-term interest. This is a careful discussion of extraordinary range and covers such developments as the move away from Mahler's early delineation of autistic and symbiotic phases, and the preverbal and subsequent development of character, behavior, and group, cultural, and familial influences on affect and the experience of affect. Blum has provided a valuable resource for any analyst or researcher on affect; while it focuses on what has developed from Mahler's work, it touches many other bases and leaves one wishing for a more thorough treatment by the author.

Similarly, in a remarkable concluding chapter, “Psychological Testing for Psychoanalysis,” Michael S. Garfinkle and Sarah Lynn Richardson sketch the development of psychological testing with an erudite, comprehensive, lightly elaborated list of contributions.

925

The Half Embrace of Psychic Bisexuality

Rajiv Gulati and David Pauley

David Pauley

401 Pacific Street, Suite B2
Brooklyn, NY 11217
E-mail: rajivgulatimd@gmail.com

401 Pacific Street, Suite B1
Brooklyn, NY 11217
E-mail: davidpauleylcsw@gmail.com

David Pauley  

bisexuality, homosexuality, transgender, Freud, Lacan, homophobia

The discourse on psychic bisexuality is explored as it unfolds in Freud's writings, in contemporary papers by LGBTQ psychoanalysts, and in a volume of essays, Psychic Bisexuality, published in 2018 by a group of prominent French and British analysts (Perelberg 2018). The greater acceptance of nonnormative sexualities and genders is reshaping the discourse on bisexuality and gender in many corners of our profession, yet, as the Perelberg volume attests, that evolution is far from universal. A strain of unquestioned heteronormativity and cisgenderism is evident in Perelberg et al.'s description of the fundamentals of psychic life, a strain that has roots (via a certain one-sided reading of Lacan) in Freud's unresolved mourning of his inchoate homosexual self. A more embracing vision of bisexuality would seek to “hold,” rather than enact, the grief and anxiety associated with the development of myriad diverse personal patterns.

Surprising Lessons from a Long-Distance Analysis

Aisha Abbasi

Aisha Abbasi

5720 Bloomfield Glens
West Bloomfield, MI 48322-2501
E-mail: abbasimd@sbcglobal.net

Aisha Abbasi  

About eighteen years ago I was doing some paperwork in my office, which at the time was in a small office building (it is now at home), when I felt the tremors of an earthquake. I left my third-floor suite, walked down the stairs to exit the building, and found its occupants huddled together outside the front door, concerned and anxious, but by now looking a bit relieved that the shaking had stopped. The building manager told us to stay outside for a while, in case there were aftershocks, so we all stayed there for another fifteen minutes or so. People started chatting with each other. Many of us did not know each other well because even though it was a small building, people came and went at different times. A few people met in the hallways occasionally, but tenants from all three floors had never been together in quite this way before.

As we were standing there chatting, people who didn't know each other began introducing themselves. Somebody asked me who I was. I told the person my name and said I was a psychoanalyst with an office on the third floor. At that moment, a young man in the group suddenly turned toward me and said, “Oh my God, you are Dr. Abbasi. I'm supposed to come see you!” I responded, “Looks like it took only an earthquake for us to meet.” He burst out laughing and then, stepping a little bit outside the group with me, explained that he had been given my name a short while ago, when he told someone he knew in the area that he wanted to be in therapy. He had been carrying my name and number around on a paper in his pocket, but had not yet gotten around to calling me. He talked about how amazing it was that my office was in the very building where he worked.

Training and Supervising Analyst, Michigan Psychoanalytic Institute.

Clinical plenary presentation, American Psychoanalytic Association, Chicago, June 18, 2016.

“The Skin is the Cradle of the Soul”: Didier Anzieu on the Skin-Ego, Boundaries, and Boundlessness

Andrzej Werbart

Andrzej Werbart

SE-106 91 Stockholm
SWEDEN
E-mail: andrzej.werbart@psychology.su.se; andrzej@werbart.se

Andrzej Werbart  

skin-ego, psychic envelopes, borderline conditions, narcissistic personalities, taboo on touching, thinking ego, limit-setting

Didier Anzieu's notion of the skin-ego builds on a long psychoanalytic tradition that began with Freud's idea that the ego is first and foremost a body ego, a projection in the psyche of the surface of the body, or, in other words, the idea that psychic phenomena are always embodied. An interface, a container for the ego, but also its origin: thus did Anzieu conceptualize the skin's psychic function. The baby's fantasy of having a common skin with the mother is the concrete starting point for a development that, through the prohibition on touching, leads to the experience of being a separate and individual person. Psychoanalytic work with severe mental disorders makes it necessary to investigate deficiencies in the skin-ego's containing function before the patient's psychic contents can be explored. In the psychoanalytic situation, the analyst's words replace tactile contact and thereby contribute to healing injuries to the skin-ego. The clinical implications of Anzieu's theoretical model are illustrated by examples from psychoanalyses of children and adults. The close connection between touch, psychic envelopes, and thinking opens a wider perspective on the necessity of setting limits to violence, against both nature and human beings.

[[RunningHead]]

Is Emerging Adulthood a New Developmental Phase?

Karen Gilmore

Karen Gilmore

305 East 18th Street
New York, NY 10003
E-mail: kjg23@cumc.columbia.edu

Karen Gilmore  

emerging adulthood, Jeffrey Arnett, twenty-first-century culture, psychoanalytic theories of development, adolescence

In 2000, Jeffrey Arnett, a developmental research psychologist, proposed a new phase of development that he called “emerging adulthood.” He delineated developmental challenges centered on identity, role exploration, and subjective experience and linked his observations to changes in the demographics and culture of contemporary society. This proposal elicited an extraordinary response in the research community, but the reaction among psychoanalysts has been tepid at best: developmental phases have not been amended for almost a century, and in some schools the very notion of such phases has been discredited. Adult development has historically attracted mostly lifespan psychoanalysts, and the concept of identity has never achieved full psychoanalytic status. But both adulthood and identity merit psychoanalytic legitimacy: adulthood because it looms in the mind as a meaningful endpoint that shapes earlier stages, and identity because it is a complex, organizing aspect of self-representation. The concept of emerging adulthood, too, has sufficient validity and heuristic value to be considered a developmental phase, provided we loosen our fixed ideas about what constitutes “developmental” and take a fresh look at the sweep of human development as it is shaping up in a transformed world.

[[RunningHead]]

Writing Gender with Sexuality: Reflections on the Diaries of Lou Sullivan

Francisco González

Francisco González

582 Market Street, Suite 305
San Francisco, CA 94104
E-mail: f.j.g@comcast.net

Francisco González  

transgender, sexuality, gender, Lou Sullivan

In contemporary psychoanalytic writing, gender tends to be disarticulated from sexuality. While this has been a theoretically useful approach, especially as regards the critical appraisal of early traditional literature (which often assumed a facile coherence between sex, sexuality, and gender), this position too often leaves gender stripped of one of the most compelling forces in psychoanalytic theorizing, namely, its relation to the sexual. Here the diaries of Lou Sullivan (1951-1991)—a transsexual man who began writing long before considering sexual transitioning—are used to present an extended example of the intimate linkage between gender and sexuality. The diaries stand as a unique historical archive: a fairly comprehensive, prospective, first-person account of transsexuality, begun before the subject self-identified as transsexual, which documents a complex and candid subjective evolution. Situated historically during a time of enormous upheaval in both psychoanalysis and the culture at large on questions of gender and sexuality, the diaries offer an additional opportunity to consider the nexus of individual psyche and social forms.

[[RunningHead]]

The Secret Life of Secrets: Deleterious Psychosomatic Effects on Patient and Analyst

Kathryn Zerbe

Kathryn Zerbe

621 SW Morrison, Suite 1000
Portland, OR 97205
E-mail: kjzerbemd@comcast.net

Kathryn Zerbe  

secret, somatic countertransference, embodied countertransference, psychosoma, embodiment, psychosomatic reaction, enactment, privacy, collusion, selected fact

The impact and complex nature of keeping secrets deserves greater scrutiny within psychoanalysis. While the capacity to keep a secret is a developmental achievement that furthers conscious choice and healthy boundary setting between self and others, an individual's need for privacy must be distinguished from untoward costs of collusion and concealment. Clinical case material shows that not all secrets are unconscious or multilayered, as assumed in most of the psychoanalytic literature. Nonetheless, in these cases deleterious effects to psyche and soma took root. These patients assumed that their secret was irreparably destructive to an essential object relationship; shame, guilt, narcissistic vulnerability, unconscious identification with an injured party, and developmental deficit were other factors found to undergird this mode of pathogenic dissembling. Two clinical examples also demonstrate that embodied countertransference reactions may herald the revelation of a secret in treatment that had been hidden, but in plain view. Secrets appear to exert their profound psychological and physical effects on patient and analyst by biological mechanisms that are as yet poorly understood but are readily observed in clinical practice. Psychoanalysts who keep in conscious awareness both the adaptive value and the potential costs of maintaining the confidences of others over the course of a career are better positioned to assist their patients and themselves in rendering essential self-care.

[[RunningHead]]

Should We Burn Melanie Klein?

Jean Laplanche

Jean Laplanche

Jean Laplanche  

Why did I choose a title that takes us back to the era of obscurantism, the dark days when the Inquisition burned people and their works? People were indeed burned, but only after great efforts had been made to make the possessed confess to a truth outside their own awareness: the truth of the devil within them.

I should start by emphasizing the extent to which the imagery of demons, witches, and possession is still with us—not only in clinical treatment, but also in psychoanalytic theory. A thesis completed by one of my students—Freud and the Devil—recently showed the extent to which such imagoes are coextensive with Freudian thinking and its development (Urtubey 1983). To be sure, a rationalist perspective belonging to so-called “Enlightenment” philosophy is another aspect of Freud's thinking: Wherever reason appears, the demons of the night should disappear forever. (Afflavit et dissipati sunt.)

But no less strong in Freud is an incorrigible enthusiasm for the devil, which resists any attempt to reduce it to an illusion. So much so that metapsychology itself, that most theoretical element of his work, is sometimes likened to a witch; I am thinking of his splendid obituary tribute to Charcot (Freud 1893).1 There Freud shows how the moment when hysterics were no longer the objects of derision marked the imminent discovery

1Editor's note. See also the following passage in “Analysis Terminable and Interminable”: “That is to say, the instinct is brought completely into the harmony of the ego, becomes accessible to all the influences of the other trends in the ego and no longer seeks to go its independent way to satisfaction. If we are asked by what methods and means this result is achieved, it is not easy to find an answer. We can only say: “So muss denn doch die Hexe dran!”—the Witch Metapsychology. Without metapsychological speculation and theorizing—I had almost said ‘phantasying’—we shall not get another step forward. Unfortunately, here as elsewhere, what our Witch reveals is neither very clear nor very detailed” (Freud 1937, p. 225).

Otto Kernberg

Otto Kernberg

21 Bloomingdale Road
White Plains, NY 10605
E-mail: okernber@med.cornell.edu

Otto Kernberg  

object relations, character pathology, psychoanalytic technique, transference structures

Definitions of specific organizations of transference developments are proposed for neurotic, borderline, narcissistic, schizoid, symbiotic, and psychotic character structures. These distinct organizations of transference developments correspond to the underlying characteristics of internalized object relations stemming from the conflictual implications of split-off, idealized, and persecutory self- and object representations. The transference structures described have implications for the corresponding application of psychoanalytic technique. Clinical cases illustrate the relationship between personality structure, transference organization, and psychoanalytic techniques.

On the Natal Body and its Confusing Place in Mental Life

Rosemary Balsam

Rosemary Balsam

64 Trumbull Street
New Haven, CT 06517
E-mail: rosemary.balsam@yale.edu

Rosemary Balsam  

procreative body, psychoanalytic body, natal body, sex and gender, resistance to female body, language for body, uterophallic vs. phallic, Kristeva, abjection, khôra

These reflections concern ongoing confusions in holding together the gestalt of the natal body, sex, gender, and procreation. Highlighting theoretical confusions is the shifting history of relations among the material body, the “psychoanalytic body,”: sex, and gender. Studying these confusions and collecting more data may help the field advance where a rush to theoretical foreclosure will not. Observe, for example, how commonly foreclosing are the terms “masculine” and “feminine” when automatically applied to the merely biological attributes of a sexed body. More confusions arise from theorizing gender identity using split-off images of body parts divorced from any natal referent. In a published clinical case, for instance, the role of the procreative body is characteristically ignored in gender theorizing. Apparently unspeakable horrors of the female body come with its birthing potential and may be a motive for erasure. Julia Kristeva, an exemplary writer, accepts simultaneously a sexed, procreative, and gendered layering in the mind. Her account of the “abject” can shed light on the suppressive horror in our field, which remains almost silent on the body's procreative potential in everyday life. By contrast, even Ice Age sculptors were engaged with procreative female bodies.

Teleanalysis: Slippery Slope or Rich Opportunity?

Lena Ehrlich

Lena Ehrlich

555 East William Street, Suite 22C
Ann Arbor, MI 48104
E-mail: lenaehrlich@comcast.net

Lena Ehrlich  

countertransference, resistance, separation, transference, teleanalysis, videoconferencing, telephone analysis, Skype, analytic frame, reluctance

In certain cases, and under certain conditions, extremely useful analytic work can be done on the phone or through videoconferencing. Contrary to what some critics of teleanalysis maintain, with patients who are motivated and can make use of analysis, physical distance between analyst and patient and/or occasional technological difficulties do not limit or preclude successful analysis. Clinical material from three teleanalyses demonstrates various conditions that help make teleanalysis useful. Instead of being a disadvantage, the juxtaposition of the experience of the “tele” and the in-person settings (in occasional in-person sessions) provides increased and unique opportunities for analysis.

[[RunningHead]]

The Draw to Overwhelm: Consent, Risk, and the Retranslation of Enigma

Avgi Saketopoulou

Avgi Saketopoulou

80 University Place, #5C
New York, NY 10003
E-mail: avgisaketopoulou@gmail.com

Avgi Saketopoulou  

overwhelm, limit consent, consent, passibility, risk, infantile sexuality, sexual, more and more, suffering pleasure, sadomasochism, perversion, SM

The concept of affirmative consent presumes a subject who is fully transparent to herself and who can anticipate the precise effects of her assent. This essay proposes limit consent, a concept that offers us different ways to think about the sexual—and about the analytic encounter. Limit consent involves a more nuanced negotiation of limits and becomes possible when the subject makes herself passible (Lyotard 1988) to an other—a condition that is neither active nor passive. Processes described in depth here suggest that passibility has ties to the rousing of infantile sexuality (Freud) and to the subject's normative perversity (Laplanche). When the psychic economy of the infantile sexual is followed to its apex, a particular kind of state is produced that I call overwhelm—a word that is used here as a noun. Overwhelm is a state of dysregulation that can be confused with, but is not the same as, repetition compulsion. Overwhelm entails risk, and under some circumstances it may open up space toward significant psychic transformations. A detailed clinical example illustrates how overwhelm may make itself known in the clinical encounter, and how it can infiltrate the transference/countertransference. Specific technical suggestions are made regarding analytic work with overwhelm.

Projective Identification in Couples

Arthur Nielsen

Arthur Nielsen

333 East Ontario Street, Suite 4209B
Chicago, IL 60611
E-mail: arthur@arthurnielsenmd.com

Arthur Nielsen  

projective identification, marriage, marital polarities, couple therapy, partner selection, mate selection

Projective identification (PI) is a complex process that can bridge the divide between individual psychodynamics and interpersonal systemic process. Consequently, it provides a powerful lens through which to examine couple conflict and unhappiness. This paper aims to clarify and demystify the concept and to illustrate its special utility for clinicians practicing individual psychoanalysis or psychotherapy, and for therapists who treat couples conjointly. It deconstructs PI into components of transference (projection), induction, and identification of both inducers and recipients; distinguishes subtypes; and then discusses some important topics surrounding the concept, including what is meant by “identification,” the importance of “containment,” and how induction is often accomplished by inaction. Clinical examples illustrate how patients use PI to manage grief, shame, past traumas, and current deficits. The utility of PI for understanding partner selection and marital polarities is illustrated, and guidelines for working with PI in psychodynamic couple therapy are provided.

[[RunningHead]]

Commentary on Laplanche

David Bell

David Bell

Flat 4 Mullion Court
112-114 Finchley Road
London NW3 5JH
UNITED KINGDOM
E-mail: davidlbell@hotmail.com

David Bell  

Laplanche's title, mysterious and playful, risks failing to signal the depth of engagement of this short paper with the work, the paradoxes, and the extraordinary achievement of Melanie Klein. His project is contextualized within the more general terrain of ruptures and tensions within psychoanalysis. So perhaps I might start with a few words in relation to this meta-position. I share with Laplanche the view that a central feature of psychoanalysis is to be found in the various tensions it contains. For example, psychoanalysis can be considered a child of the Enlightenment even while announcing a new major obstacle to its continued development. One can also think of this as the tension between the two forms of modernism it embraces1; there is the new knowledge it makes possible and the sober recognition of the limitations we labor under to gain access to this knowledge. Then there is that conflict between the more humanistic and scientistic poles that strain our subject, maintaining the many-sidedness of its object. But these tensions are always, I believe, under danger of collapse—leading to simplification and degradation in psychoanalytic thinking. In this sense we might distinguish two kinds of rupture: there is a progressive form of rupture that demands a break with previous modes of thought; this form will always present itself to some extent as a threat (here Laplanche has Klein firmly situated); then there is another kind of rupture that is regressive—here there is an abandonment of the tensions just discussed and a collapse into one pole or the other (for example, psychoanalysis reconfigured as a purely mechanistic science or, alternatively, as only a humanistic discipline of interpretation and meaning).

1The term modernism has various meanings; here I am referring to (a) modernism in the sense of enlightenment and (b) the modernism, often referred to as “high modernism,” that characterized the radical ruptures in modes of thought in the move from the nineteenth to the twentieth century.

Free Speech, Love Speech, Hate Speech, and Neutrality: In and Out of the Consulting Room

Donald Moss

Donald Moss

80 University Place
New York, NY 11201
E-mail: dbmossmd@gmail.com

Donald Moss  

My patient, a thirty-five-year-old man from the Midwest, new to New York: “Okay. It's summer. I was just waiting outside for the session. All these women with yoga clothes on. Perfect butts. You don't get those butts by accident. What do they want from me? I'm not safe anywhere. I'm beleaguered. I'm reduced. I see them and suddenly I'm no ego, just wanting. That one and that one and that one. No matter, twelve years old, twenty-two, fifty-two. It's not decent. I can understand the Taliban. Sitting out there, I had my Taliban moment. Just cover up, why don't you? Why don't all of us, men, women, all of us, just cover the fuck up. Leave me alone. Let me be. I don't want to be this way. I can't stand it. I see them and I want it all. I don't want to want what I want. Father, forgive me.”

For a hundred and twenty-five years and more, we analysts have done our best to deal with “love speech” like this—its too much-ness, its power to overwhelm, to paralyze and to reduce both its speakers and its listeners, to drive us all crazy, to turn tenderness into cruelty, desire into violence, yearning into flight. (One could reasonably call love speech “erotic speech,” but for purposes of contrast and clarity here, “love speech” seems to me the more apposite term.)

Love speech is transmitted in the voice of the first-person singular, “I” my patient says, and means it. In spite of what he calls his “Taliban moment,” he makes it clear that it is not all men, but instead he and he alone here who is suffering.

Unaffiliated with an institute; co-founder of Green Gang, a four-person collective working with climate change denial and the relation between the human and the natural worlds; Chair, Program Committee, American Psychoanalytic Association.

Commentary on González

Adrienne Harris

Adrienne Harris

80 University Place, Fifth Floor
New York, NY 10003-4564
E-mail: adrienneharris@gmail.com

Adrienne Harris  

Reading Francisco González's engaging and demanding paper, winner of the 2017 Ralph Roughton Award, I remembered the fresh, intense experience of hearing an earlier version, given at a conference, a meeting wryly titled “Masculinity, Complex.” The play and demand of that title remain very alive in this more theorized version of González's project. Here González undertakes innovative theory in entangling gender with sexuality, doing so through a trip into the archives. The clinical material is drawn from transman Lou Sullivan's diaries, written over a thirty-year period beginning in the early 1960s.

The “Masculinity, Complex” conference was held in New York in 2012. Even after the short intervening period of six years, it feels as if the worlds and words about gender and sexuality have been dramatically revised (and continue to be revised) in deep and powerful permutations. This paper breaks new ground in requiring that its readers view the complex mutative engagement of sexuality with gender through psychoanalytic, sociocultural, and historical lenses. I want to notice and differentiate, as González does, the sociocultural forces and particular specificity of history.

In my discussion I will focus on four issues: (1) gender with sexuality as fluid and as fixed; (2) the social, the cultural with the intrapsychic, and the particularity of history: (3) models of complexity for subjectivity; and (4) translation and writing.

Gender with Sexuality as Fluid and as Fixed

In his essay, González insists that we keep sexuality and gender in “complex stratification[s]” (p. 60). Might we also say: imbricated, intersectionally engaged, softly assembled?

Faculty, NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis.

Ira Brenner   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Jefferson Meical College; Training and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Center of Philadelphia.

This book is part of the series Studies in the Psychosocial, edited by Stephen Frosch, Peter Redman, and Wendy Hollway. These three scholars, all British academics, define their mission thus: “to investigate the ways in which psychic and social processes demand to be understood as always implicated in each other, as mutually constitutive, co-produced, or abstracted levels of a single dialectical process. As such, it can be understood as an interdisciplinary field in search of transdisciplinary objects of knowledge. Studies in the Psychosocial is also distinguished by its emphasis on affect, the irrational, and unconscious processes, often but not necessarily, understood psychoanalytically” (p. ii). (The disclaimer at the end of this statement, regarding unconscious processes, frankly puzzles me.)

Stepping up to the challenge in this volume is Karl Figlio, Professor Emeritus at the University of Essex, Senior Member of the Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy Association of the British Psychoanalytic Council, and an Associate of the British Psychoanalytical Society. He further establishes his bona fides by acknowledging Robert Hinshelwood as a friend and colleague, as well as Ronald Britton, “who has informed every level of my thinking” (p. vii). By creating this link to two prominent neo-Kleinians, we are assured that Figlio will have done his psychoanalytic homework in tackling the task at hand.

In his introduction, the author argues that historical consciousness is the common denominator of psychoanalysis and history and that the two are thus “rooted in the same soil” (p. 1).

Roy Schafer, trained as a psychologist, was one of the best teachers of psychoanalytic ideas our field has ever produced. In addition to being a brilliant scholar, an engaging writer, and a fund of immense creativity, he was a practitioner of the clinical art and science of daily work in the office. His mind was extraordinarily expansive, restless to critique and improve the theory and technique of analytic work and to elaborate ever deepening understandings of the mind.

A Couple State of Mind: Psychoanalysis of Couples and the Tavistock Relationships Model is a tour de force. Many readers of this journal may be unfamiliar with the literature on analytic couple therapy. This book is

710

an up-to-date place to start, a landmark for couple analytic therapists but also a volume to orient the individual analyst to the relevance of this application of psychoanalysis.

Written by Mary Morgan, a psychoanalyst, couple psychoanalytic psychotherapist, and member of the International Committee of Couple and Family Psychoanalysis of the International Psychoanalytical Association, the book is a major contribution to the literature. Morgan investigates the methodology of psychoanalytic couple therapy that originated and developed over seventy years at the Tavistock Clinic (now Tavistock Relationships), and compares these advances with couple psychoanalytic currents occurring beyond the United Kingdom, and between individual psychoanalysis and analytic couple therapy.

The reader will be impressed not only by the rigor of Morgan's scholarly enterprise, but also by the humility, grace, and generosity that those who know her personally will recognize. Here she shares the full range of her original ideas regarding the couple relationship and couple analytic process. Like our moment-to-moment breathing that goes on outside our awareness, we employ Morgan's terms as if we have always had them, forgetting sometimes to credit their inventor. For example, such concepts as the “creative couple” (Morgan 2005), and “projective gridlock” (Morgan 1995) are products of her innovative mind informed by various post-Kleinian influences. The very title of the book—A Couple State of Mind—is an idea conceived by Morgan in 2001, and is thus particularly fitting as a unifying framework for her work with couples.

The book comprises ten chapters, packed with clinical vignettes artfully selected to illustrate complex dynamics and techniques. Commencing with an historical overview of the Tavistock Relationships theoretical approach, Morgan then lays down foundational ideas for establishing a couple analytic setting, conducting an assessment, and engaging the couple in treatment. The next section delves into theory and technique to orient the therapist working with a distressed couple: individual/joint unconscious fantasy, shared defense, “unconscious beliefs” (Britton 1995), transference and countertransference, and the couple projective system. Morgan then takes up the particularly disturbed dynamic at work with narcissistically organized couples and the ways they relate to their shared psychic space. She also explores the couple's psychic development and matters relating to gender and sexuality.

Salman Akhtar

Salman Akhtar

833 Chestnut East 210-C

Salman Akhtar  

Elsewhere I have said that upon being “asked to identify the five most influential theorists in our field since Freud, most well-informed psychoanalysts would come up with the names of Melanie Klein, Donald Winnicott, Wilfred Bion, André Green, and Otto Kernberg. Occupying a place of great distinction, Kernberg is the pre-eminent psychoanalytic theoretician of our times” (Akhtar 2012, p. 995). In contributions spanning well over fifty years, he has painstakingly and consistently sought to integrate diverse conceptual strands in psychoanalysis, forge links between descriptive psychiatry and metapsychology, underscore the pitfalls of customary psychoanalytic education, shed light on problems of love, hate, and mourning, and delineate the intricate ways in which psychoanalytic technique can be modified and adapted for the treatment of patients with severe psychopathology. Kernberg's depth of knowledge, his breadth of concern, and a literary style that combines the precision of a surgeon and the parsimony of a banker makes it hard to “discuss” a piece of his writing, let alone “critically evaluate” it. Add to this the personal element of my great debt, both conceptual and professional, to him for his innumerable acts of kindness toward me and you will get some sense of the daunting nature of my task here. But forge ahead I must and say a few things about this most recent contribution of his.

Kernberg's Main Points
